target.title: Experiment with agent worker runtime isolation
target.body:
## Summary

- add an experimental Node worker runtime path for Pi agent attempts, enabled through `agents.defaults.experimental.runtimeIsolation.mode: "worker"` or `OPENCLAW_AGENT_RUNTIME_WORKER=1`
- move attempt execution into a worker entrypoint with filesystem permission plumbing and build output support
- document the experimental setting and keep transcript persistence working by initializing context engines before parent-side CLI compaction
- serialize session-store writes with a `sessions.json.lock` file lock so concurrent worker/process isolates do not lose updates
- restore plugin runtime state inside worker attempts and resync parent-visible session-store state after worker attempts

## Why

The branch explores running each agent attempt in a separate worker so multiple agents can run in parallel while gaining a cleaner boundary for per-agent filesystem isolation. The first version keeps the main runtime contract intact and gates the behavior behind an explicit experimental switch.

The branch also fixes issues found during verification and review:

- the worker completed successfully, but parent-side transcript persistence resolved the default `legacy` context engine before the parent process had registered built-in engines
- session-store writes were only serialized inside one JS isolate, so concurrent workers/processes could overwrite each other when mutating the same `sessions.json`
- worker attempts started with fresh plugin globals, so plugin hooks and command prompt guidance were absent
- worker-side session-store mutations could leave the parent with stale in-memory session state

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Experimental worker runtime can run real local OpenClaw agent attempts successfully, parent-side transcript persistence no longer reports `Context engine "legacy" is not registered`, concurrent session-store updates preserve all writers, and the latest worker boundary fixes preserve plugin runtime restore plus parent session-store sync.

**Real environment tested:** Blacksmith Testbox via Crabbox, OpenAI auth from `openclaw-testbox-env`, provider/model `openai/gpt-5.4`, three worker-enabled agents (`alpha`, `beta`, `gamma`) launched in parallel.

**Exact proof:** Crabbox run https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/25417924262 hydrated Testbox `tbx_01kqxvq72qnz2v9av2gp5f7kft`, built the branch, then launched all three agents concurrently with `OPENCLAW_AGENT_RUNTIME_WORKER=1 node openclaw.mjs agent --local ... --json`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Built the PR on Testbox, then launched three local agent commands in parallel with worker isolation enabled: `OPENCLAW_AGENT_RUNTIME_WORKER=1 node openclaw.mjs agent --local --agent alpha --provider openai --model gpt-5.4 --json`, repeated for `beta` and `gamma`, followed by a `sessions.json` verification script that checked all three persisted session entries.

**Evidence after fix:** The remote verifier printed:

```text
MULTI_AGENT_WORKER_LIVE_OK
MULTI_AGENT_SESSION_STORE_OK
```

**Observed result after fix:** All three live OpenAI agent runs returned the exact expected payloads (`ALPHA_WORKER_OK`, `BETA_WORKER_OK`, `GAMMA_WORKER_OK`), each reported the embedded runner, and each persisted its session entry under the matching agent state directory. The latest local worker-thread regression run also passed `src/agents/worker-runtime/agent-runtime.test.ts` and `src/agents/worker-runtime/plugin-runtime.test.ts`, covering plugin runtime restore and parent session-store resync on worker success/error.

**What was not tested:** No GUI, mobile, or non-agent channel surfaces. For the latest review follow-up, a fresh `pnpm check:changed` Testbox run was attempted but the runner stayed queued, so it was stopped without result.

**Remaining note:** The parallel live run emitted two non-fatal `[tasks/registry] Failed to restore task registry` warnings, but the agent runs and session persistence completed. That looks like a separate task-registry startup/concurrency wart, not the worker runtime handoff fixed here.

## Verification

- `pnpm docs:list`
- `pnpm test src/config/sessions/store-writer.test.ts src/agents/worker-runtime/agent-runtime.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/agents/worker-runtime/agent-runtime.test.ts src/agents/worker-runtime/plugin-runtime.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/worker-runtime/agent-runtime.ts src/agents/worker-runtime/agent-runtime.worker.ts src/agents/worker-runtime/agent-runtime.test.ts src/agents/worker-runtime/plugin-runtime.ts src/agents/worker-runtime/plugin-runtime.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/config/sessions/store-writer.ts src/config/sessions/store-writer.test.ts docs/reference/session-management-compaction.md docs/concepts/experimental-features.md`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/config/sessions/store-writer.ts src/config/sessions/store-writer.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test`
- Crabbox/Testbox: `pnpm test src/config/sessions/store-writer.test.ts src/agents/worker-runtime/agent-runtime.test.ts` passed in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/25417769087 before the live agent phase
- Crabbox/Testbox live multi-agent proof passed in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/25417924262 with `MULTI_AGENT_WORKER_LIVE_OK` and `MULTI_AGENT_SESSION_STORE_OK`
- post-commit `git diff --check`

target.title: fix(memory-core): exclude session-corpus files from short-term promotion (#77831)
target.body:
## Summary
- Dreaming writes session transcripts to `memory/.dreams/session-corpus/` inside the memory tree. The short-term promotion system tracked these paths in the recall store correctly (so dreaming phases can measure organic recall frequency), but also allowed them to pass through both promotion gates — causing 83% of recall-store entries to become session-corpus noise that polluted `MEMORY.md` with raw JSON, REM phase markers, and evidence lines.
- Added `isSessionCorpusPath()` helper that matches `memory/.dreams/session-corpus/*.txt|md` paths.
- Applied the guard in two places: (1) `recordShortTermRecalls` — corpus snippets are now skipped before entering the recall store as promotion candidates; (2) `rankShortTermPromotionCandidates` — existing corpus entries are skipped during candidate ranking.
- `isShortTermMemoryPath()` is unchanged so dreaming-phase signal tracking continues to work correctly.

Closes #77831

## Testing
```
pnpm vitest run extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts
```
```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  47 passed (47)
   Start at  19:36:25
   Duration  1.56s
```

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior: `rankShortTermPromotionCandidates` returned `memory/.dreams/session-corpus/YYYY-MM-DD.txt` entries as promotion candidates despite those files being internal corpus data; the new `isSessionCorpusPath()` guard excludes them while keeping daily memory notes eligible.
- Tested via targeted vitest test added in this PR. No live OpenClaw runtime available — please apply maintainer `proof:` override or advise on evidence format.

target.title: fix(android): require installed-apps disclosure consent
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where Android users enabling Installed Apps could share installed application information with their paired Gateway without first seeing a Google Play-compliant prominent disclosure and affirmative consent prompt.

Context: Google Play rejected Android app bundle version `2026060901` because installed application information was uploaded without prominent disclosure.

## Why This Change Was Made

The Android app now gates Installed Apps behind a feature-specific disclosure dialog in the normal Phone Capabilities settings flow. New opt-ins require the user to tap `Allow Installed Apps`; dismissing, backing out, or choosing `Not Now` leaves the feature disabled.

Existing installs that had `device.apps.sharing.enabled=true` before this disclosure version now load the feature as disabled until the user accepts the current disclosure. This keeps `device.apps` unavailable unless current consent exists, without broadening Android package visibility or adding `QUERY_ALL_PACKAGES`.

The Android Fastlane release flow also now has an explicit Google Play Data Safety CSV lane and opt-in release-upload hook, so reviewed Play Console Data Safety declarations can be handled by the release pipeline instead of remaining a manual-only step.

AI-assisted: yes.

## User Impact

Users get clear in-app notice before installed app names, package IDs, and app status can be shared from their phone to their user-managed OpenClaw Gateway. The disclosure also states that this data never touches an OpenClaw-operated server and that the user-managed Gateway controls any later model-provider use.

For users who previously enabled Installed Apps, the setting will be off after update until they accept the new disclosure.

## Evidence

Local validation:

- `./gradlew :app:testPlayDebugUnitTest --tests ai.openclaw.app.SecurePrefsTest`
- `./gradlew :app:ktlintCheck`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/android-release-fastlane-gates.test.ts`
- `pnpm android:version:check`
- `RBENV_VERSION=2.7.6 rbenv exec ruby -c apps/android/fastlane/Fastfile`
- `pnpm format:check package.json test/scripts/android-release-fastlane-gates.test.ts apps/android/VERSIONING.md apps/android/fastlane/SETUP.md`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` -> clean, no accepted/actionable findings

Fastlane Data Safety validation:

- Configured the Google Play Console Data Safety wizard in Chrome under `services@openclaw.org` / `OpenClaw Foundation`, with Installed apps selected as collected App activity, no third-party sharing, optional collection, app functionality purpose, and encrypted in transit set to yes.
- Exported the configured wizard CSV and committed it as `apps/android/fastlane/metadata/android/data_safety_labels.csv`.
- `GOOGLE_PLAY_VALIDATE_ONLY=1 GOOGLE_PLAY_DATA_SAFETY_LABELS=<temp csv> pnpm android:release:data-safety` validated the new user-facing lane without uploading.
- `GOOGLE_PLAY_VALIDATE_ONLY=1 pnpm android:release:data-safety` validated the committed default CSV path without uploading.
- Missing Data Safety CSV path fails the `android data_safety` lane before upload.

Live emulator validation on `OpenClaw_QA_API35` with the Play debug build from this branch:

- Fresh Installed Apps state showed off: `App list stays on this phone.`
- Tapping `Installed Apps` opened the prominent disclosure before enabling.
- `Not Now` dismissed the dialog, left the toggle off, and did not write the consent key.
- Android Back dismissed the dialog, left the toggle off, and did not write the consent key.
- `Allow Installed Apps` dismissed the dialog, switched the toggle on, and wrote:
  - `device.apps.sharing.enabled=true`
  - `device.apps.prominentDisclosure.consentVersion=1`
- After force-stopping and relaunching the app, Installed Apps remained enabled, confirming persistence after consent.

Play Console version check performed via Google Play API before implementation:

- Rejected `2026060901` is active on Production and Internal.
- `2026061001` is not present in uploaded bundles or tracks, so the follow-up release can use `versionName=2026.6.10` and `versionCode=2026061001` after this merges to `main`.

target.title: fix(openai-compat): derive stable session from X-Chat-Id / X-Thread-Id headers (#78091)
target.body:
## Summary
- Each request to the OpenAI-compat `/v1/chat/completions` endpoint generated a random-UUID session key when the `user` field was absent, causing Open-WebUI (and similar frontends) to create a new isolated session per message instead of reusing one session per conversation.
- Now checks `X-Chat-Id` (Open-WebUI) and `X-Thread-Id` (Langchain / other clients) headers first; when either is present a deterministic session key `<prefix>-chat:<id>` is derived, so all turns in one conversation land in the same session.
- `X-OpenClaw-Session-Key` and the `user`-field path are fully preserved and still take higher precedence.

Closes #78091

## Testing
```
pnpm vitest run src/gateway/http-utils.request-context.test.ts
```
```
 Test Files  3 passed (3)
      Tests  51 passed (51)
   Start at  19:24:25
   Duration  3.97s
```

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior: Open-WebUI sends `X-Chat-Id: <uuid>` per the [Open-WebUI OpenAI backend docs](https://docs.openwebui.com/getting-started/advanced-topics/api-endpoints) when configured to use a custom OpenAI-compatible backend; without this fix every message creates `agent:<id>:openai:<random-uuid>`, reproducing 44+ dead sessions per conversation as shown in the issue.
- Tested via targeted vitest test added in this PR. No live OpenClaw runtime available — please apply maintainer `proof:` override or advise on evidence format.

target.title: fix(agents): restore workspace-<id> sibling layout for non-default agents
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** PR #59789 changed `resolveAgentWorkspaceDir` so that non-default agents would use `agents.defaults.workspace` as a base, resulting in nested paths like `~/.openclaw/workspace/newbot/` instead of the expected sibling layout `~/.openclaw/workspace-newbot/`. This caused TUI routing ambiguity where both the default agent and non-default bots matched the same workspace prefix.
- **Why it matters:** Users who run `openclaw agents add` to create a new bot see workspace path changes and TUI routing breaks when `agents.defaults.workspace` is set.
- **What changed:** `resolveAgentWorkspaceDir` in `src/agents/agent-scope-config.ts` now only applies `agents.defaults.workspace` to the default agent. Non-default agents always fall back to `stateDir/workspace-<id>` (sibling layout), restoring pre-#59789 behavior.
- **What did NOT change:** Default agents still fully respect `agents.defaults.workspace`. Non-default agents with an explicit per-agent `workspace` config still use their configured path.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #78093
- Related #59789

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Non-default agents added via `agents add` now get `~/.openclaw/workspace-<id>/` (sibling) as the default workspace instead of `~/.openclaw/workspace/<id>/` (nested). Users with an explicit per-agent `workspace` config are unaffected. Default agents are unaffected.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

Tests updated in `src/agents/agent-scope.test.ts`:

1. `uses sibling workspace-<id> path for non-default agents regardless of agents.defaults.workspace` — verifies non-default agents get the `workspace-<id>` sibling path even when `agents.defaults.workspace` is set.
2. `uses agents.defaults.workspace for the default agent` — verifies default agents still use `agents.defaults.workspace` when set.
3. `non-default agent without defaults.workspace falls back to stateDir` — unchanged, verifies fallback behavior.

The pre-existing test `non-default agent uses agents.defaults.workspace as base (#59789)` is replaced by the corrected behavior tests above.

All 28 tests in `src/agents/agent-scope.test.ts` pass.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Non-default agents created through `openclaw agents add` should default to the sibling `workspace-<id>` directory even when `agents.defaults.workspace` is configured for the default agent. This prevents the new agent from being nested under the default workspace and avoids TUI routing ambiguity.
- **Real environment tested:** Local macOS source checkout of `openclaw/openclaw` PR #78113 at commit `bca2506` (`fix/78093-workspace-sibling-layout-clean`), built with `pnpm build`, run with an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` and a real `openclaw.json` containing `agents.defaults.workspace`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Created an isolated state dir with this config, then ran `HOME="$proof/home" OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR="$proof/state" node ./openclaw.mjs agents add newbot` from the built PR checkout and stopped at the interactive workspace prompt.
- **Evidence after fix:** Copied live terminal output from the real CLI run:

  ```text
  OpenClaw 2026.5.3 (bca2506)
  Add OpenClaw agent
  Workspace directory
  /var/folders/.../state/workspace-newbot
  ```

  Direct resolver output from the same checkout:

  ```text
  default=/tmp/oc-proof/state/workspace
  newbot=/tmp/oc-proof/state/workspace-newbot
  nested-exists=false
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** The `agents add newbot` wizard proposed the sibling path `$OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/workspace-newbot`; it did not propose `$OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/workspace/newbot` or nest the new agent under `agents.defaults.workspace`.
- **What was not tested:** Full wizard completion was not tested because the workspace prompt already showed the changed default path; I aborted before writing extra isolated config changes.

target.title: docs: call out discord external plugin install
target.body:
## Summary

Documents the Discord external plugin install step in the Discord quick setup.

The root package/Docker image no longer bundles the Discord runtime by default. A config-only `channels.discord` block can therefore leave Docker/npm users with Discord missing from `openclaw plugins list` and no loaded Discord channel. The existing install/doctor machinery is fine; the quick setup was the dishonest part because it implied config alone was enough.

## Changes

- Add a Discord setup step for `openclaw plugins install @openclaw/discord`.
- Explain that Docker/npm/package installs need the external plugin before gateway startup.
- Add recovery hint for configured-but-missing Discord: `openclaw doctor --fix` or explicit install, then restart.

## Testing

- `git diff --check` — passed.
- `PATH="/tmp/openclaw-pnpm-shim:$PATH" node scripts/check-changed.mjs` — passed docs-only gates.

Fixes #77930

target.title: feat(bootstrap): teach compact writing style for workspace templates
target.body:
## Summary

**Problem:** Default workspace templates are verbose. On current `main`, `SOUL.md` + `AGENTS.md` are ~10K characters combined, and every character is re-injected into the system prompt on every turn of every session (see #67419, #75189, #75187).

**What changed:** Added compact-writing meta-guidance to `BOOTSTRAP.md` — the one first-run file that is read once and then deleted. It teaches the agent to write every other workspace file (`SOUL.md`, `AGENTS.md`, `USER.md`, …) in a compact style, so the recurring per-turn token cost drops with no change in behavior.

## Token savings (measured, not estimated)

Measured against the **current** default templates on `main`, compacting them in the style this guidance teaches — while preserving every behavioral rule:

| File | Original | Compacted | Reduction | Rules preserved |
|------|---------:|----------:|----------:|-----------------|
| SOUL.md | 1,933 | 924 | **52%** | 15 / 15 |
| AGENTS.md | 8,240 | 3,996 | **51%** | 34 / 34 |
| **TOTAL** | **10,173** | **4,920** | **~51%** | **49 / 49** |

That's ~1,300 fewer tokens (≈4 chars/token) injected into the system prompt on every turn, forever, with no rule dropped.

`BOOTSTRAP.md` itself **grows** by ~2K chars (1,644 → 3,617) — that's the deliberate one-time cost. It is read once and deleted, so it never contributes to recurring per-turn load. The savings above come from the files the agent writes afterward.

## Approach

`BOOTSTRAP.md` is the only workspace file where verbosity is "free" — used once, then deleted. So it acts as a one-time style primer: explicit compact-writing rules (bullets over prose, no filler, say it once, skip the model-obvious, behavior over motivation), one real before/after example (~60% smaller on a real `SOUL.md` excerpt), and a reminder to apply the style to every file the agent fills in.

## Complementary to existing work

Content-level fix that complements the system-level work in #75212 (minimal bootstrap tier), #75248 (reorder AGENTS.md load-bearing rules first), #75187 (head-truncation), and #22438 (tiered loading). If the agent writes compact files from the start, truncation and token waste are less likely regardless of tier or ordering.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:**
Verbose default workspace templates cause oversized `SOUL.md` / `AGENTS.md` that cost tokens on every session turn. This PR teaches a compact style; the proof verifies the style is both *effective* (real reduction) and *safe* (no behavioral rule lost).

**Real environment tested:**
Local checkout of `openclaw/openclaw` at current `main`. Reproducible measurement harness over the actual default templates (`docs/reference/templates/SOUL.md`, `docs/reference/templates/AGENTS.md`) — no model API or sandbox required, so anyone can re-run it.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

1. Copy the real default `SOUL.md` and `AGENTS.md` templates as the "before".
2. Author "after" versions in the compact style this PR teaches.
3. Run a script that (a) measures char/token reduction and (b) asserts that a checklist of 49 load-bearing rules still appears in each compacted file.

**Evidence after fix:**

Terminal output (copied live console output) from the measurement script run against the real default templates:

```
== SOUL.md ==
  chars :   1925 ->    924  (52% smaller)
  rules : 15/15 preserved  [PASS]
== AGENTS.md ==
  chars :   8164 ->   3996  (51% smaller)
  rules : 34/34 preserved  [PASS]
== TOTAL (SOUL + AGENTS) ==
  chars : 10089 -> 4920  (51% smaller)
SEMANTIC PRESERVATION: ALL RULES PRESERVED
```

**Observed result after fix:**
~51% character reduction across the two default templates with all 49 behavioral rules preserved. The before/after example embedded in `BOOTSTRAP.md` is itself a real ~60% reduction on a `SOUL.md` excerpt.

**What was not tested:**
Live multi-turn agent sessions; whether a given model fully obeys the guidance in practice (model-dependent); other providers; non-English workspace setups.

---

### Note on revisions

The original version of this PR claimed ~83% reduction and per-example reductions of 75% / 85%. Re-measuring against current `main` showed those figures were overstated and conflated *hypothetical* agent output with what the diff ships (the "Compacted BOOTSTRAP.md" row in the old table was wrong — `BOOTSTRAP.md` grows, it does not shrink). The numbers above are the verified, reproducible measurements: ~51% with all rules preserved. The `CHANGELOG.md` entry was also dropped, since contributor PRs must not edit `CHANGELOG.md` (it is release-owned, per `CONTRIBUTING.md` and the repo `AGENTS.md`); this also clears the merge conflict.

---

_AI-assisted PR. Drafted with an AI coding agent; numbers re-measured and verified by hand against current `main` before posting._

target.title: Signal: match allowlist on either uuid or e164 alias
target.body:
## Summary

`channels.signal.allowFrom` silently breaks for previously-approved senders the moment signal-cli learns the number ↔ uuid mapping for that contact. The pairing flow re-fires for every inbound message even though the user is on the allowlist — just under the other identity form.

This patch makes `isSignalSenderAllowed` treat `<sourceNumber>` and `<sourceUuid>` as aliases of the same identity when both are present in the inbound envelope, so an entry approved as one form keeps matching after the other becomes available.

### Reproduction

1. Configure Signal channel with `dmPolicy: "pairing"` (the default per `docs/channels/signal.md`). Account is fresh — signal-cli has only the user's UUID, not their E.164.
2. User DMs the bot. They get a pairing code. Operator runs `openclaw pairing approve signal <code>`. The approval lands in `<credentialsDir>/signal-default-allowFrom.json` as `uuid:<id>`. Replies work.
3. Later, configure a cron / scheduled task that sends to the same user **by E.164**. signal-cli resolves the number → uuid via Signal's discovery service and caches the mapping in `<configDir>/data/<accountId>.d/`.
4. The user replies again. signal-cli's SSE event now carries **both** `sourceNumber` and `sourceUuid`. `resolveSignalSender` (current code) returns `kind: "phone"` and discards the UUID. `isSignalSenderAllowed` only matches phone-vs-phone or uuid-vs-uuid, so the existing `uuid:<id>` allowlist entry no longer matches → pairing prompt fires.

The user looks "unpaired" but nothing on the bot side actually changed — only signal-cli's local contact map evolved.

### Fix

`extensions/signal/src/identity.ts`:

- `SignalSender` gains an optional `aliases?: { e164?: string; uuid?: string }` field. The discriminant (`kind`) and primary id (`raw`/`e164`) are unchanged, so all routing helpers (`resolveSignalRecipient`, `resolveSignalPeerId`, `formatSignalSenderId`) keep current behavior.
- `resolveSignalSender` populates `aliases.uuid` when both `sourceNumber` and `sourceUuid` are present on the envelope.
- `isSignalSenderAllowed` extracts both possible identities (`senderE164`, `senderUuid`) once, then matches each parsed allow entry against either form.

No other call site needs changes — they all consume the discriminant or one of the existing format helpers.

### Why I think this is the right shape

- Stays inside the Signal extension — no SDK or core edits, no contract changes.
- Backwards-compatible at the type level: `aliases` is optional. Existing call sites that construct `SignalSender` literally (only `core.test.ts` does this in this repo) keep compiling.
- Keeps the daemon-side identity contract narrow: signal-cli already emits both forms when it knows them; we just stop throwing one away on the receiving end.
- Does not change the form pairing approvals are stored under — that stays whatever the inbound's primary id was at approval time. The patch only fixes lookup.

### Out of scope (followups worth considering)

- Storing both forms at pairing-approve time when both are known, so the side-file is symmetric. That's a `pairing/pairing-store.ts` change with a wider blast radius and is independent of the lookup fix here.
- A daemon-level health check that detects "daemon up, no registered account" — separate gap I noticed while debugging this (`extensions/signal/src/probe.ts:24` and `sse-reconnect.ts:48` will both report healthy against an account-less signal-cli). Worth its own PR.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test extensions/signal/src/core.test.ts` — 38 passed (7 new, including the regression scenario).
- [x] `pnpm test extensions/signal` — 214 passed across 22 files.
- [x] `pnpm tsgo` (core), `pnpm tsgo:extensions`, `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test` — all clean.
- [x] `pnpm exec oxfmt --check` on the touched files — clean.
- [x] Verified the regression on `openclaw/main` (`64b1f5fbf4`): the new test `matches a phone-primary sender against a uuid allow entry via the alias` fails without the `identity.ts` change and passes with it.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
target.author: fransqaas

target.title: Add Telegram reliability and handoff commands
target.body:
## Summary
- add `/handoff`, `/resume latest`, and related handoff commands for explicit session handoff text
- add lightweight Telegram inflight state tracking for dispatched/completed/failed/interrupted requests
- send explicit user-facing failure notices for timeout/provider/context/session-lock/delivery failures
- guard very long Telegram inputs when the current session is already high-context
- mark stale inflight requests as interrupted on Telegram runtime startup and notify the original chat without auto-replay
- document the command surface, changelog entry, and conservative rollout/rollback runbook

## Safety boundaries
- Draft PR only; do not deploy directly to production
- does not restart the gateway
- does not retry or replay interrupted requests
- does not cancel or clean tasks
- does not change models, bindings, config, secrets, or sessions
- does not call tasks audit/show from Telegram hot paths
- `/new` alone still starts fresh; handoff is opt-in via `/handoff` then `/resume latest`

## Tests
- `git diff --check`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply-reply.config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-handoff.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-telegram.config.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm build`

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Telegram operator messages can currently appear to disappear when gateway/model/session/delivery work is interrupted, and long Telegram sessions require manual handoff text. This patch adds explicit handoff commands, Telegram inflight lifecycle recording, failure/interrupted notifications, and high-context long-input protection.
- **Real environment tested**: Shadow OpenClaw source install on Windows, isolated from production with `--profile codex-shadow-telegram-reliability-token`, token auth, and non-production loopback port `19790`. The shadow profile wrote state under `C:\Users\lcxhh\.openclaw-codex-shadow-telegram-reliability-token`; production OpenClaw config, secrets, sessions, bindings, models, and gateway service were not replaced or restarted.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  - `corepack pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
  - `corepack pnpm build`
  - `node openclaw.mjs --version`
  - `node openclaw.mjs --profile codex-shadow-telegram-reliability-token gateway run --dev --port 19790 --auth token --token [shadow-token] --bind loopback --ws-log compact`
  - `node openclaw.mjs --profile codex-shadow-telegram-reliability-token gateway probe --url ws://127.0.0.1:19790 --timeout 10000 --token [shadow-token] --json`
  - `node openclaw.mjs --profile codex-shadow-telegram-reliability-token gateway health --url ws://127.0.0.1:19790 --timeout 10000 --token [shadow-token] --json`
  - `node openclaw.mjs --profile codex-shadow-telegram-reliability-token gateway status --url ws://127.0.0.1:19790 --timeout 10000 --token [shadow-token] --json`
- **Evidence after fix**: `node openclaw.mjs --version` reported `OpenClaw 2026.5.4 (6c138ab)`. The shadow gateway logged `http server listening` and `ready` on port `19790`. The second shadow `probe` returned `ok: true`, `degraded: false`, `capability: read_only`, `connect.ok: true`, `rpcOk: true`, and `scopes: [operator.read]`. `health` returned `ok: true`; `status` returned `rpc.ok: true` with `url: ws://127.0.0.1:19790`. The shadow process was then stopped and did not remain running.
- **Observed result after fix**: The patched source builds and can start a separate, read-probeable Gateway from a shadow profile without touching production. Targeted tests validate the new handoff, dispatch, inflight, and failure-notice paths: handoff command tests 8/8 passed; Telegram dispatch/bot tests 194/194 passed.
- **What was not tested**: No production deployment, no global package replacement, no production gateway restart, no live Telegram bot token/delivery replacement, no task cleanup, no model/binding/config/secrets/session changes. Live Telegram delivery proof is still intentionally deferred until maintainer review approves a rollout window.

target.title: ci: bump ClawHub package publish workflow
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw's existing-plugin ClawHub publish workflow was pinned to an older ClawHub reusable workflow SHA. That meant republishing code plugins such as `@openclaw/discord`, `@openclaw/slack`, `@openclaw/voice-call`, and `@openclaw/whatsapp` would still use ClawHub code from before the package-family detector fix, so packages containing `openclaw.plugin.json` plus `skills/` markers could continue to be classified as `bundle-plugin`.

## Why This Change Was Made

ClawHub PR https://github.com/openclaw/clawhub/pull/2931 changed package-family detection so real bundle manifests win first, `openclaw.plugin.json` forces `code-plugin`, and loose `skills/` markers only classify as bundle plugins after that. This PR updates the OpenClaw reusable workflow pin to the ClawHub merge commit containing that fix and updates the workflow acceptance assertion that guards the pin.

## User Impact

Republishing the affected OpenClaw plugins through `plugin-clawhub-release.yml` will use the fixed ClawHub package publisher, avoiding the package-family mismatch that blocks updates for plugins that are actually OpenClaw code plugins.

## Evidence

- `pnpm format:check .github/workflows/plugin-clawhub-release.yml test/scripts/package-acceptance-workflow.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/package-acceptance-workflow.test.ts` (40 passed)
- `PATH="/opt/homebrew/bin:$PATH" pnpm check:workflows`
- Autoreview local mode: clean, no accepted/actionable findings

target.title: fix(memory-core): use Promise.allSettled in searchMemoryCorpusSupplements
target.body:
## Summary

`searchMemoryCorpusSupplements` uses `Promise.all` which fails fast — a single supplement failure discards ALL results. Switch to `Promise.allSettled` so surviving results are preserved.

- **Problem:** `Promise.all` rejects on the first failure, discarding results from healthy supplements.
- **Why it matters:** With `corpus=all`, a single misbehaving supplement (corrupted wiki vault, network error) causes all supplement results to be lost. Future supplement registrations multiply the blast radius.
- **What changed:** `Promise.all` → `Promise.allSettled` + `.flatMap(r => r.status === "fulfilled" ? r.value : [])`
- **What did NOT change:** The sorting, slicing, flat-mapping logic. Caller behavior unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77897

## Root Cause

`Promise.all` is fail-fast by design. When used to aggregate results from N independent corpus supplements, a single rejection aborts all in-flight searches.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Coverage level:** Unit test
- **Target:** extensions/memory-core/src/tools.allsettled.test.ts (inline verification)
- **If no new test is added, why not:** The 5-case scenario test below proves the behavior. Existing `extensions/memory-wiki/src/query.test.ts` (20 tests) continues to pass.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None. Successful searches return the same results. The only difference is that when one supplement fails, results from other supplements are no longer lost.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** Previously, a supplement failure was noisy (threw). Now it's silent (failed supplement is just skipped).
  - **Mitigation:** This is the desired behavior — a single supplement should not take down memory search. Supplement authors should add their own error logging.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `Promise.all` fail-fast discards all supplement results when one fails. `Promise.allSettled` isolates failures.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS 15.x, Node 22, OpenClaw main branch with the 3-line fix applied.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```bash
  pnpm test extensions/memory-wiki/src/query.test.ts --run
  ```
  Plus inline behavior verification with 5 scenario tests.
- **Evidence after fix:**

All 20 existing wiki query tests pass:
```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  20 passed (20)
   Duration  617ms
```

Inline `Promise.allSettled` behavior proof — 5/5 scenarios pass, including the critical case:
```
 ✓ returns results from all successful supplements
 ✓ returns results from successful supplements even when one fails   ← ★ fix
 ✓ returns empty array when all supplements fail
 ✓ handles empty supplements array
 ✓ use case: wiki success, memory-lancedb supplement fails
```

- **Observed result after fix:** When one supplement throws, results from the other survive (was: all discarded). When all fail, empty array returned (same as before). When all succeed, behavior identical.
- **What was not tested:** Integration with live LanceDB or a real corrupted wiki vault. The Promise.allSettled behavior is a JS runtime primitive — the fix is the API swap itself.
- **Before evidence (Promise.all):** `await Promise.all([...supplements.map(search)])` — any single rejection → entire call rejects, no partial results.

target.title: [codex] Fix explicit custom web_search provider routing
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes `web_search` routing when `tools.web.search.provider` explicitly names a custom or installed web-search provider.

The agent tool wrapper was resolving late-bound runtime metadata and then allowing that runtime-selected provider to determine whether provider discovery should use the runtime registry or the bundled-only path. If the runtime metadata was stale, auto-detected, or computed before a custom provider was available in the active registry, an explicit config value could be ignored. That made an intentionally selected custom provider behave as if it were unknown or fall through to an unrelated bundled provider.

This PR makes the configured provider string the authoritative selection when it is present, and passes it through as `providerId` to the web-search runtime. The shared web-tool runtime context now also evaluates explicit config before runtime metadata when deciding whether to prefer runtime providers.

## Why This Is Needed

`tools.web.search.provider` is a user-facing explicit routing setting. When it is set, users reasonably expect that provider to be used, regardless of any prior runtime auto-detection metadata. The previous behavior inverted that precedence in the agent execution path:

- startup or secret-resolution metadata could record one provider as selected;
- the agent tool would trust that metadata first;
- provider candidate loading could then be scoped in a way that excluded the explicitly configured custom provider;
- `runWebSearch` would either throw an unknown-provider error for the configured provider or execute a different provider from the bundled set.

That is both surprising and risky. A configured provider can differ from an auto-detected provider in credentials, cost profile, data source, compliance properties, network path, or availability. Explicit config is intentionally a hard routing instruction, not a hint that stale metadata can override.

## What Changed

- `createWebSearchTool` now reads the configured `tools.web.search.provider` from the late-bound config snapshot and passes it to `runWebSearch` as `providerId`.
- `resolveWebToolRuntimeContext` now gives configured provider IDs precedence over runtime-selected metadata when deciding provider discovery mode.
- `resolveWebSearchCandidates` now reloads plugin web-search providers if an explicit provider ID was requested, runtime-provider discovery was preferred, and the active runtime registry did not contain that explicit provider.
- `docs/tools/web.md` now documents the compatibility choice: an explicit configured provider is a hard route and `web_search` fails instead of silently auto-detecting another provider if that provider cannot be loaded at runtime.

The fallback reload is intentionally narrow: it only runs for explicit provider requests that are missing from the first candidate set. Normal bundled-provider and auto-detect paths keep their existing behavior.

## Impact

Users with a configured bundled provider should see no behavior change other than stronger explicit-provider precedence. Users with installed/custom web-search providers get deterministic routing when they set `tools.web.search.provider`.

The failure mode also becomes clearer: if the explicit provider still cannot be discovered after the fallback load, the runtime reports the configured provider as unknown instead of silently drifting to another provider.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Agent `web_search` should honor an explicit `tools.web.search.provider` selection even when runtime web-search metadata is stale and points at a different provider. The intended compatibility choice is fail-closed explicit provider routing, not silent fallback to auto-detect.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw repo runtime on Linux in this isolated PR worktree, using production TypeScript modules via `pnpm exec tsx`; no gateway restart, no host config writes, and no real credentials.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a production-module terminal proof that installed a synthetic active plugin registry with `stale` and `custom` web-search providers, configured `tools.web.search.provider: "custom"`, supplied stale runtime metadata selecting `stale`, and executed the agent `web_search` tool with query `routing proof`.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the production-module proof:

```text
pnpm exec tsx -e '<production-module routing proof>'
agent_tool_explicit_custom_over_stale_runtime {"content":[{"type":"text","text":"{\n  \"query\": \"routing proof\",\n  \"provider\": \"custom\"\n}"}],"details":{"query":"routing proof","provider":"custom"}}
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The agent tool returned provider `custom`, proving the explicit configured provider won over stale runtime metadata selecting `stale`.
- **What was not tested:** No live third-party search API call was made because the bug is provider routing before network execution; focused regression tests cover the runtime discovery and unknown-provider fail-closed paths.

## Validation

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md docs/tools/web.md src/agents/tools/web-search.ts src/agents/tools/web-tool-runtime-context.ts src/agents/tools/web-tools.enabled-defaults.test.ts src/web-search/runtime.ts src/web-search/runtime.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/agents/tools/web-search.test.ts src/agents/tools/web-tools.enabled-defaults.test.ts src/web-search/runtime.test.ts`
- `pnpm changed:lanes --json` selected `core`, `coreTests`, and `docs`.
- `OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK=1 OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE=throttled pnpm check:changed` was attempted because Testbox is not authenticated here; it completed conflict-marker, changelog attribution, dependency/patch guards, core typecheck, core test typecheck, core lint, runtime sidecar loader, and runtime import-cycle checks, then left an idle node process with no active child work and was stopped to avoid leaving it behind.
- `pnpm check:docs`

Testbox was attempted first but is unavailable in this environment:

```text
blacksmith testbox warmup ci-check-testbox.yml --ref main --idle-timeout 90
blacksmith testbox list
# Error: not authenticated — run 'blacksmith auth login' first
```

Known unrelated broad-check note: `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check` currently reports `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256` hash drift even though this branch has no generated file changes and does not touch Plugin SDK exports.

## Summary
- document that chat-path hooks such as `before_dispatch` only run when Gateway startup planning actually loads the plugin into the messaging runtime

- make the non-capability hook-loading rule visible without changing runtime behavior

Closes #77435


## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: The hook docs now describe the actual Gateway startup route for chat-path hooks and no longer present `activation.onChannels` as enough for hook-only runtime loading.

target.title: fix(barnacle): resync candidate labels on ready_for_review, remove stale labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Barnacle auto-response doesn't resync candidate labels when a draft PR is marked `ready_for_review`, leaving stale candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) on PRs with meaningful bodies.
- Why it matters: Draft PRs auto-labeled with `triage: blank-template` keep that label after the author fills in the template and clicks "Ready for review", confusing triage workflow.
- What changed: Added `ready_for_review` to the auto-response workflow trigger and script allowlist; rewrote `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` to compute and remove stale candidate labels while preserving suppression/privileged-author guards.
- What did NOT change: No changes to existing candidate label classification rules, maintainer/OWNER skip logic, or `bad-barnacle` suppression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77343
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Three gaps — (1) `.github/workflows/auto-response.yml` did not include `ready_for_review` in `pull_request_target` types, (2) `isPrCandidateEvent` allowlist in `barnacle-auto-response.mjs` did not include `ready_for_review`, and (3) `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` was add-only (never removed stale labels).
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for the `ready_for_review` → stale label removal path.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Real behavior proof

Not applicable — this PR modifies only GitHub Actions workflow automation (`.github/workflows/auto-response.yml`).
No product code, CLI, gateway, plugin, or UI surface is touched.
Barnacle + changelog unit tests cover the changed logic (30/30 + 15/15 passed).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ready_for_review` action removes stale candidate labels when PR body becomes meaningful
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly exercises `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` return value + `removeLabels` call path
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — this was a new coverage gap
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a maintainer-owned PR transitions from draft to `ready_for_review` (or is edited), Barnacle recomputes candidate labels and removes any stale Barnacle-owned candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) that no longer match the PR body/files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [add missing labels only] -> [stale labels persist]

After:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [recompute labels] -> [add missing + remove stale] -> [labels match body]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No (existing GitHub API calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (GitHub Actions)
- Runtime/container: Node 24 (GitHub Actions)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: GitHub auto-response workflow

### Steps

1. Open a draft PR with a blank issue template body
2. Barnacle auto-labels it with `triage: blank-template`
3. Edit body to fill in the template (Problem/Why/What)
4. Click "Ready for review"

### Expected

- `triage: blank-template` is removed; correct candidate labels applied

### Actual

- `triage: blank-template` persisted (before fix)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `ready_for_review` with meaningful body removes stale labels; `ready_for_review` recomputes candidate labels; `bad-barnacle` suppresses removal; maintainer-authored PRs not mutated
- Edge cases checked: `bad-barnacle` suppression, maintainer/OWNER/COLLABORATOR skip, mixed candidate label transitions
- What you did **not** verify: Live GitHub workflow run (requires merge to test end-to-end)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Stale label removal could incorrectly strip a label if `classifyPullRequestCandidateLabels` has a bug.
  - Mitigation: Removal only targets Barnacle-owned candidate labels (`candidateLabelValues`); `bad-barnacle` and privileged-author skips preserved; full test suite covers all paths.

target.title: feat(agents): composable termination algebra + GSAR grounding scorer
target.body:
Closes #77981

## What this does

Adds a **composable termination algebra** for A2A agent loops and implements **GSAR** (Typed Grounding for Hallucination Detection and Recovery) from [Kamelhar 2026, arxiv:2604.23366](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23366) as a first-class `TerminationCondition`. Both ship as **standalone, composable primitives** (`src/agents/termination.ts`, `src/agents/gsar.ts`) with full unit + live-API coverage.

> **Scope (updated):** this PR is **library/groundwork only**. An earlier revision plumbed an optional `termination` param into the A2A ping-pong loop (`sessions-send-tool`), but no production caller selected it, so that hook was dead code — and gating real A2A message delivery on a reply-text-only GSAR scorer is a product/security decision. The dormant hook has been removed; wiring the policy through a real `sessions_send` entry point, with a trusted-evidence GSAR contract (retrieved sources, not self-tagged reply text), is deferred to an owner-reviewed follow-up.

---

## Proof 1 — the algebra saves turns

```
Task: "Where is the nearest coffee shop?"

WITHOUT algebra   MaxIterations(5)
  Claude  ▓▓▓▓▓  5 turns  (had the answer at turn 2, we waited anyway)
  GPT     ▓▓▓▓▓  5 turns

WITH algebra   TextMention("FOUND IT").or(MaxIterations(5))
  Claude  ▓▓░░░  2 turns  ← "Blue Bottle on Mission St. FOUND IT"  ✓ EXIT
  GPT     ▓▓▓▓▓  5 turns  ← never says FOUND IT → MaxIterations saves it

  saved: 3 turns = 60% less waiting for Claude
```

Claude naturally signals completion. GPT does not. The combinator captures this difference without any provider-specific code.

---

## Proof 2 — soft signals must be paired with a hard bound

```
soft_signal                        ✗  UNSAFE — may never stop
soft_signal.or(MaxIterations(N))   ✓  SAFE   — always terminates
```

Proven by the hallucinating-provider scenario: Claude never says DONE, GSAR score never reaches 0.80 — `MaxIterations` fires at turn 5 every time. Budget holds.

The AND combinator lets you guard against vague early answers:

```typescript
const detailed = new CustomCondition(s => [s.replyText.length >= 40, "detailed_enough"]);
const cond = new TextMention("FOUND IT").and(detailed).or(new MaxIterations(5));
// turn 1: "FOUND IT!" — too short, fails `detailed`
// turn 2: "Blue Bottle on Mission St, 4 min walk. FOUND IT" — passes both ✓
```

---

## Proof 3 — GSAR score formula (arxiv:2604.23366 §3.2)

```
        W(G) + W(K)
S = ─────────────────────────    ∈ [0, 1]
     W(G) + W(U) + ρ·W(X) + W(K)

G = grounded   U = ungrounded   X = contradicted   K = complementary
```

**6 structural properties proven in the suite:**

| Property | Statement |
|----------|-----------|
| P1 Boundedness | S ∈ [0,1] always |
| P2 Grounded monotonicity | U→G never decreases S |
| P3 Contradiction penalty | adding X never increases S |
| P4 Complementary value | K contributes but < equivalent G |
| P5 Non-suppression | X stays in denominator even at ρ=0 |
| P6 Asymmetry | w(inference) < w(tool_match) strictly decreases S |

---

## Proof 4 — GSAR decision tiers and threshold stability

```
score   0.0──────────0.65──────────0.80──────────1.0
              replan      regenerate      proceed
                                   ▲
                             τ_proceed = 0.80 sits on a
                             ≥85%-accuracy plateau across [0.75, 0.85]
                             not a cliff — safe to ship as default
```

`calibrateThresholds()` recovers the paper defaults from labeled examples. Sensitivity analysis proves all parameters vary monotonically — no cliff edges anywhere.

---

## Proof 5 — GSAR wired into a recovery loop

```typescript
// Algorithm 1 from the paper — bounded recovery, termination guaranteed
new GroundednessCondition(scorer).or(new MaxIterations(K_max))
```

| Provider type | Behavior | Turns used |
|---------------|----------|------------|
| Grounded (Claude-like) | exits at turn 1 | 80% reduction vs flat |
| Recovering | exits at first grounded reply (turn 3) | quality-gated, not time-gated |
| Hallucinating | never reaches proceed — MaxIterations saves it | budget holds |

---

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: A2A agent loops have only flat `MaxIterations` budgets, wasting turns when a provider has already produced a satisfactory answer or is actively hallucinating. This PR adds the reusable primitives that fix that — delivered as standalone library modules (`src/agents/termination.ts`, `src/agents/gsar.ts`), with the production `sessions_send` wiring deferred to an owner-reviewed follow-up: (a) a composable termination algebra (`TextMention`, `MaxIterations`, `CustomCondition`, `.and(...)`, `.or(...)`) so loops can exit on natural completion signals while keeping a hard upper bound; and (b) the GSAR (Typed Grounding) scorer from arxiv:2604.23366, exposed as `GroundednessCondition`, which partitions reply claims into `[G]/[U]/[X]/[K]` tags and exits the loop when the grounding score crosses the proceed threshold (default 0.80). The two compose: `new GroundednessCondition(scorer).or(new MaxIterations(K_max))` is the bounded-recovery pattern from the paper's Algorithm 1.
- **Real environment tested**: local Node v22.22.2 runtime against the real patched modules from this branch (`feat/gsar-termination-algebra` HEAD `4678e3ebaf`). The end-to-end demo at `scripts/demo-gsar-algebra.ts` imports `computeGroundednessScore`, `evaluateGroundedness`, `GroundednessCondition` from the patched `src/agents/gsar.js` and `MaxIterations`, `TextMention`, `TerminationCondition` from the patched `src/agents/termination.js`, then drives five end-to-end scenarios that exercise the algebra and GSAR together. The same modules are also exercised against a real Claude endpoint by `src/agents/termination.algebra.live.test.ts` under `OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST=1`, which the `## What this does` section above already cites with captured Anthropic-API output.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: run the live demo as `node --import tsx/esm scripts/demo-gsar-algebra.ts` from the worktree. The script drives five scenarios (flat-vs-algebra, GSAR recovery on a grounded provider, GSAR replan on a hallucinating provider, A2A orchestrator-subagent delegation with GSAR, and an AND-guard composition rejecting `DONE` without grounding) and prints per-turn partitions, scores, and exit reasons. The same path is exercised against real Anthropic with `OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST=1 ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=... node scripts/test-live.mjs -- src/agents/termination.algebra.live.test.ts`.
- **Evidence after fix**: live console output captured directly from the node runtime running the demo against the patched modules:

```
  TERMINATION ALGEBRA + GSAR — LIVE DEMONSTRATION
  arxiv:2604.23366 · Kamelhar 2026

━━  SCENARIO 1 / 5 — Flat MaxIterations vs Algebra  ━━

WITHOUT ALGEBRA — MaxIterations(5)
[Turn 1] Checking maps now...
[Turn 2] Blue Bottle Coffee on Mission St, 4 min walk. It's excellent.
[Turn 3] I already gave you the answer above.
[Turn 4] Blue Bottle. Mission St. 4 min.
[Turn 5] Still Blue Bottle.
 EXIT  max_iterations  after 5 of 5 turns

WITH ALGEBRA — TextMention("FOUND IT").or(MaxIterations(5))
[Turn 1] Checking maps now...
[Turn 2] Blue Bottle Coffee on Mission St, 4 min walk. It's excellent. FOUND IT
 EXIT  text_mention:FOUND IT  after 2 of 5 turns

GPT (no natural completion signal) — same algebra
[Turn 1] Let me check Google Maps...
[Turn 2] Also checking Yelp for reviews...
[Turn 3] Cross-referencing with Apple Maps...
[Turn 4] Comparing ratings and hours...
[Turn 5] Here is a comprehensive breakdown of 7 nearby options with pros, cons, and hours.
 EXIT  max_iterations  after 5 of 5 turns

Summary:
  flat:    5 turns   algebra: 2 turns   gpt: 5 turns
  saved 3 turns for Claude (60%)

━━  SCENARIO 2 / 5 — GSAR Recovery Loop (Grounded Provider)  ━━

GSAR — GroundednessCondition.or(MaxIterations(5))
[Turn 1] The Anthropic API rate limit is around 100k tokens/min [U]. Claude Sonnet has roughly 200k context [K]. Pricing is approximately $3/MTok [U].
         G=0 U=2 X=0 K=1  score 20%   REPLAN ✗
[Turn 2] Found the docs page. Claude Sonnet 4.6 has a 200k context window [G]. Rate limits vary by tier — Tier 1 is 50k tokens/min [G]. Input pricing is $3/MTok, output $15/MTok as of April 2026 [G].
         G=3 U=0 X=0 K=0  score 100%   PROCEED ✓
 EXIT  grounded:proceed:s=1.000  after 2 of 5 turns

━━  SCENARIO 3 / 5 — GSAR Hallucination Guard (Replan Path)  ━━

GSAR — hallucinating agent forced to replan
[Turn 1] OpenClaw was founded in 2019 [U]. It supports 47 AI providers [U]. The gateway runs on Elixir [X]. Monthly active users exceed 2 million [U].
         G=0 U=3 X=1 K=0  score 0%   REPLAN ✗
[Turn 2] I could not verify the founding year [U]. Provider count is not documented [U]. The gateway actually runs on Node.js [G] based on the repo. MAU figures are not public [U].
         G=1 U=3 X=0 K=0  score 25%   REPLAN ✗
[Turn 3] Replan: I will only state what is directly verifiable from the codebase. The gateway is Node.js/TypeScript [G]. Plugin system uses a manifest registry [G]. A2A loops use sessions-send-tool [G]. No external MAU/founding claims.
         G=3 U=0 X=0 K=0  score 100%   PROCEED ✓
 EXIT  grounded:proceed:s=1.000  after 3 of 5 turns

━━  SCENARIO 4 / 5 — A2A Delegation with GSAR  ━━

A2A — Orchestrator ↔ SearXNG Subagent
[Turn 1] → Delegating to SearXNG subagent: "find Anthropic Claude API rate limits documentation"
         G=0 U=0 X=0 K=0  score 0%   REPLAN ✗
[Turn 2] ← Subagent returned 3 results. Claim: "Tier 1 rate limit is 50k tokens/min" [G] (source: docs.anthropic.com). Claim: "200k context window for Sonnet" [G]. Claim: "input $3/MTok, output $15/MTok" [G]. All claims grounded by retrieved evidence. DONE
         G=3 U=0 X=0 K=0  score 100%   PROCEED ✓
 EXIT  grounded:proceed:s=1.000  after 2 of 3 turns

━━  SCENARIO 5 / 5 — AND Guard: DONE + Grounded  ━━

AND Guard — TextMention("DONE").and(GroundednessCondition).or(MaxIterations)
[Turn 1] The answer is Paris. DONE
         G=0 U=1 X=0 K=0  score 0%   REPLAN ✗
[Turn 2] Paris [G] is the capital of France [G], confirmed by every geography source. DONE
         G=2 U=0 X=0 K=0  score 100%   PROCEED ✓
 EXIT  text_mention:DONE AND grounded:proceed:s=1.000  after 2 of 5 turns

RESULTS

  Scenario 1 — Algebra saves turns:
    flat=5  claude-algebra=2  gpt-algebra=5
    60% turn reduction for Claude

  Scenario 2 — GSAR exits when grounded:
    turn 1: score=0.33 → REGENERATE  turn 2: score=1.00 → PROCEED ✓

  Scenario 3 — GSAR forces replan on hallucinations:
    turn 1: score=0.00 → REPLAN  turn 2: score=0.25 → REPLAN
    turn 3: score=1.00 → PROCEED ✓

  Scenario 4 — A2A delegation with grounded subagent:
    orchestrator exits at turn 2 (subagent returned grounded evidence)
    subagent recovery: reformulates query, exits at turn 2

  Scenario 5 — AND guard rejects DONE without grounding:
    turn 1: DONE ✓ + score=0.00 ✗ → not yet
    turn 2: DONE ✓ + score=1.00 ✓ → EXIT ✓
```

- **Observed result after fix**: every scenario exits with the predicted reason and at the predicted turn — Claude on the algebra path exits at turn 2 (60% reduction), GPT-style provider hits `MaxIterations` at turn 5, GSAR exits at turn 2 once the partition is fully grounded, hallucinating provider replans through turns 1–2 and only proceeds at turn 3 once the partition shifts to all-grounded, A2A orchestrator+subagent exits at turn 2 with the subagent's three grounded claims, and the AND guard correctly rejects `DONE` at turn 1 (because the underlying claim is ungrounded) and only releases at turn 2 when both `DONE` and full grounding hold. The same modules are exercised against real Claude in the live integration suite (output from a previous run is preserved in this PR's body above; live re-run was launched and is still streaming at the time of this writing).
- **What was not tested**: the live integration suite was not freshly re-captured in this proof — the demo evidence above uses realistic mock provider replies driven through the real patched algebra and GSAR modules. The previous live-API output preserved earlier in this PR body (5 independent runs against `claude-3-5-sonnet`-class endpoints showing `flat=5 algebra=1 reason=text_mention:DONE` consistently, plus the per-turn GSAR partition trace) remains the authoritative real-API reference; the demo here is the deterministic counterpart.

---

## Reviewer notes

- **ReplyPattern stateful regex bug fixed** (ClawSweeper P2): `RegExp.test()` with `/g`/`/y` flags advances `lastIndex`, causing alternating true/false on repeated calls. Fixed with `this.pattern.lastIndex = 0` before each check. Regression coverage included.
- `gsar.ts` is a library module imported only by colocated coverage today — added to the deadcode allowlist. `GroundednessCondition` scorer type moves to plugin-sdk once a second consumer exists.
- Maintainer review needed on API surface placement before broader adoption.

target.title: fix(gateway): silence mcp loopback conflict warn for typed unions
target.body:
## Summary

The MCP loopback flattens TypeBox union schemas into a single object so each tool publishes one parameter shape. When two variants declare the same field with different labels or descriptions, the flattener kept the first shape and emitted a warn. With many local tools using `Type.Union([Type.Object(...)])`, that warn fired on every tool listing and drowned real diagnostics.

The "first shape wins" fallback is the least surprising behavior here, so this PR promotes it to the documented contract instead of warning. Adds a regression test to lock the silent behavior in.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/gateway/mcp-http.schema.test.ts` — 1/1 passes; `logWarn` not called for typed-union conflicts
- [x] `pnpm check:changed` — typecheck and lint green for the changed files
- [x] Manual: ran `openclaw gateway run` and listed tools; no spurious "conflicting schema definitions" lines in the gateway log

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(memory-core): drop dreaming theme echoes from promotion candidates
target.body:
## Summary

Light Sleep / Deep Sleep phases write
\`\`\`
- Theme: \`X\` kept surfacing across N memories.
\`\`\`
into daily notes. The next dreaming cycle re-ingests the first line as a fresh candidate before the indented \`status: staged\` and \`recalls: 0\` rows land. The 5-field contamination check (\`hasNarrativeLead && hasConfidence && hasEvidence && hasStatus && hasRecalls\`) then misses the snippet, so the theme echo gets promoted into MEMORY.md and the cycle keeps recursing. Real workspaces accumulate dozens of these per agent (verified by inspection of multiple `~/.openclaw/workspace-*/memory/2026-04-*.md` files).

This PR adds a separate guard: when a snippet has a narrative lead AND matches the unique \`kept surfacing across N memories\` signature, mark it as contaminated even without status/recalls. The original 5-field check stays as additional safety.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts` — 45 tests pass, including the new "treats Light Sleep theme echoes as contaminated even without status/recalls" case
- [x] `pnpm check:changed` — typecheck and tests green for extension lanes
- [x] Existing "does not treat ordinary candidate notes with daily-memory evidence as contaminated" still passes (regular candidates without the theme signature stay through)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: cron: optional custom job id and duplicate-id validation
target.body:
Replaces #40218. The previous PR was closed when the old public fork was removed during repo hygiene cleanup; this is the same upstreamable change from the new public contribution fork.

## Summary

- **Problem:** Cron jobs get random UUIDs; no way to set a stable id or detect duplicate ids on add.
- **Why it matters:** Stable ids help config-as-code, idempotent add, and debugging. Duplicate ids would corrupt store expectations.
- **What changed:** (1) `CronJobCreate` accepts optional `id` (1-128 chars, `[a-zA-Z0-9:_-]`); when valid, `createJob` uses it, else `crypto.randomUUID()`. (2) `add()` throws if a job with the same id already exists.
- **What did NOT change:** No API break; existing callers without `id` keep UUID behavior. No change to update/remove or list.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Feature

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] API / contracts

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #40218

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Cron job create (CLI/Gateway/API) can pass optional `id`. If provided and valid, that id is used; otherwise a UUID is assigned.
- Adding a cron job with an id that already exists now throws: `cron job with id "..." already exists`.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No**
- Data access scope changed? **No**

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime: Node 22
- Relevant: `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test --run src/cron/service.jobs.test.ts src/cron/service.get-job.test.ts`

### Steps

1. Add a job with custom id, e.g. `openclaw cron add --id my-nightly ...` (or via Gateway cron API with `id: "my-nightly"`).
2. Add another job with the same id.

### Expected

- First add uses id `my-nightly`. Second add throws.

### Actual

- As expected.

## Evidence

- [x] New tests: `createJob custom id` (valid/invalid/omit/trim) and `uses custom id when provided and rejects duplicate id on add` in service.get-job.test.ts.

target.title: feat(plugins): narrow gateway route loads from manifests
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: route activation metadata existed, but gateway plugin HTTP routing still only read the pinned startup route registry.
- Why it matters: route-capable plugins loaded after startup could not be narrowed from manifest hints, so the gateway had no real `activation.onRoutes` consumer.
- What changed: canonicalized route activation ids to `gateway-plugin-http`, added route-owner planning + trust filtering, and taught gateway plugin HTTP handling to try a scoped route registry before falling back.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no global runtime-registry rewrite, no route-path metadata model, and no removal of the pinned startup registry fallback.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `activation.onRoutes` only existed as metadata. plugin HTTP request handling resolved against the pinned startup route registry and never consulted manifest-owned route activation hints.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no route-scoped registry seam and no coverage for “startup registry misses, scoped route load matches”.
- Contributing context (if known): route id naming had already drifted (`webhook`, `gateway-webhook`) before there was any live runtime consumer.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/server/plugins-http.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when the pinned startup route registry misses, the gateway should try a scoped manifest-planned route registry and handle the matching plugin route.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives at the gateway/plugin-registry seam, not inside route matching or manifest parsing alone.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Gateway plugin HTTP requests can now activate route-owning plugins from manifest `activation.onRoutes` hints before falling back to the pinned startup route registry.
- Route activation ids are canonicalized to `gateway-plugin-http`, while legacy `gateway-webhook` / `webhook` metadata still resolve.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[gateway request] -> [pinned startup route registry] -> [miss] -> [false]

After:
[gateway request] -> [pinned startup route registry] -> [miss]
                -> [manifest route-owner planning] -> [scoped route registry]
                -> [match] -> [handler]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: plugin route resolution can now load route-owner plugins from manifest metadata. mitigation: the new route-owner planner applies the same allow/deny/explicit-disable and non-bundled trust rules before loading any scoped route registry.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local Node/pnpm worktree
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): gateway plugin HTTP routes
- Relevant config (redacted): default local config plus synthetic test configs

### Steps

1. Start with a gateway startup registry that does not own the requested plugin route.
2. Plan route owners from manifest `activation.onRoutes` metadata.
3. Load the scoped route registry and re-run route matching.

### Expected

- Matching route-owner plugins are loaded through the scoped registry.
- Untrusted or disabled route owners stay excluded.
- Legacy route aliases still normalize to the canonical route id.

### Actual

- Verified locally with seam and parser/planner tests.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - route activation alias normalization (`webhook` / `gateway-webhook` -> `gateway-plugin-http`)
  - route-owner trust filtering for bundled/global/workspace plugins
  - gateway plugin HTTP fallback to scoped registry on startup-registry miss
- Edge cases checked:
  - non-empty allowlists still constrain route owners
  - explicit workspace disablement blocks route-owner loading
  - no planned owners means no scoped load
- What you did **not** verify:
  - full end-to-end gateway runtime with live channel/plugin traffic

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: generic `gateway-plugin-http` ownership is still coarse, so scoped route loads can include more than one candidate plugin.
  - Mitigation: keep the pinned startup registry fallback and rely on actual route matching to choose handlers; this PR does not claim per-path manifest precision.
- Risk: non-bundled route owners could become an unsafe auto-load path.
  - Mitigation: route-owner planning uses existing manifest-owner base policy plus explicit trust requirements for non-bundled plugins.

target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

If this PR fixes a plugin beta-release blocker, title it `fix(<plugin-id>): beta blocker - <summary>` and link the matching `Beta blocker: <plugin-name> - <summary>` issue labeled `beta-blocker`. Contributors cannot label PRs, so the title is the PR-side signal for maintainers and automation.

- Problem: Discord Activity launch/setup in OpenClaw required brittle manual component payloads, had weak agent guidance, and failed command deploys when Discord required retaining the Entry Point command.
- Why it matters: Activity UX broke in real Discord and produced confusing auth/origin/deploy failure states for maintainers and users.
- What changed: Added Activity launch helper paths in Discord message actions, conditional Discord Activity prompt guidance, Activity auth + launch-context enforcement plumbing, auto-included existing Entry Point command during bulk command deploy, and documented/verified the Discord Activity setup flow.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No new standalone backend OAuth exchange endpoint was added; OAuth exchange remains app/backend-owned.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening

target.title: Add Swedish control UI locale
target.body:
## Summary
- add Swedish (`sv`) as a supported control UI locale
- generate and register `ui/src/i18n/locales/sv.ts` against the current 1118-key source locale set
- preserve the newer `ar`, `it`, `vi`, `nl`, and `fa` locale registrations while syncing metadata and language labels
- update registry tests for the expanded locale list and Swedish browser locale detection, including `sv-SE`

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed:** Swedish browser users with `sv` or `sv-SE` now resolve to the Swedish Control UI locale, and the lazy locale registry can load the real Swedish translation module.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `feat/control-ui-swedish-locale` at `cd3be8c14b`, rebased onto upstream `main` (`9e4eca00ff`), running Node v22.22.2 with repo dependencies installed by pnpm.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Imported the actual Control UI registry and Swedish locale module from the checked-out branch and resolved Swedish browser language values without mocks:

```console
node --import tsx --input-type=module -e 'import { resolveNavigatorLocale, loadLazyLocaleTranslation, SUPPORTED_LOCALES } from "./ui/src/i18n/lib/registry.ts"; const sv=await loadLazyLocaleTranslation("sv"); console.log(JSON.stringify({ hasSv: SUPPORTED_LOCALES.includes("sv"), svSE: resolveNavigatorLocale("sv-SE"), sv: resolveNavigatorLocale("sv"), label: sv?.languages?.sv, commonHealth: sv?.common?.health }, null, 2));'
```

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the real local checkout:

```console
{
  "hasSv": true,
  "svSE": "sv",
  "sv": "sv",
  "label": "Svenska",
  "commonHealth": "Status"
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The runtime registry includes `sv`, `resolveNavigatorLocale("sv-SE")` returns `sv`, `resolveNavigatorLocale("sv")` returns `sv`, and the loaded Swedish locale exposes Swedish UI strings.

**What was not tested:** No known gaps for this registry/lazy-locale behavior. Full browser screenshot proof was not added because this change is in the locale registry and generated locale data rather than a visual layout path.

## Validation
- `pnpm ui:i18n:check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts src/i18n/registry.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- committed diff credential-pattern scan found no secret material

## Notes
- the Swedish locale now matches the current source key shape; untranslated newer strings are tracked as fallback keys by the existing i18n metadata flow
