target.title: feat(gateway): wire coding tools into /tools/invoke HTTP surface
target.body:
> **Update 2026-06-29 — head `9f4b13fff10`: fix the `host_write_allow` audit-detail overstatement (ClawSweeper [P1]/[P2]).** The finding interpolated the real allowlisted subset (`writeToolsInAllow`) in its opening clause but then hardcoded "the `write` and `edit` coding tools" as exposed — so a subset config (`hostFsWrite: true` + `gateway.tools.allow: ["edit"]`) was wrongly told the sibling `write` primitive is also reachable. Now the exposure clause names only the allowlisted write-class tool(s) (`src/security/audit-gateway-config.ts`). Added a regression test asserting the `edit`-only detail names `edit` and **not** `write`. Verified in the `node:22-bookworm-slim` arm64 container — red/green: against the prior code the new test fails (`expected … not to contain \`write\``); with the fix it passes. Full gate green: audit + `tools-invoke-http` 176/176 + `schema.help.quality` 23/23, `tsgo:core` clean, oxlint clean. The only remaining item is maintainer security/product acceptance of the host-FS direct-invoke boundary.

---

> **Update 2026-06-28 — head `448d4813e8f`: fix the plugin-name compatibility on the REAL plugin path (addresses ClawSweeper [P1]).** The previous fix preserved a same-named plugin only at the final gateway deny filter, but the production plugin loader filters `pluginToolDenylist` names first — so a same-named plugin `read`/`write`/`edit` was still dropped before that filter (the bot was right; the prior unit proof passed only because the mocked `createOpenClawTools` ignored the denylist). Fix: in `src/gateway/tool-resolution.ts`, the host-FS coding names are now excluded from the `pluginToolDenylist` handed to the loader **when they are denied only by the host-FS default** (explicit `gateway.tools.deny` / owner-only still deny the plugin); the built-in stays gated at the final filter. The test mock now **mirrors the production loader** (drops a plugin-owned tool whose name is in `pluginToolDenylist`), so the regression tests exercise the real path. Verified in the `node:22-bookworm-slim` arm64 container — **red/green**: reverting the exclusion makes the two plugin-compat tests fail with `expected 404 to be 200`; with the fix they pass. The real resolver output was confirmed directly (the `pluginToolDenylist` it passes excludes `read`/`write`/`edit` in the host-FS-default case, includes a name under explicit `gateway.tools.deny`). Full gate green: `tools-invoke-http` 176/176, `audit-gateway-tools-http` 19/19, `schema.help.quality` 23/23, `plugin-sdk-surface-report --check` clean, **`tsgo:core` typecheck clean** (also fixed pre-existing `defaultGatewayDeny`/`ownerOnlyGatewayDeny` `.includes(string)` type errors my earlier `tsc` check had missed), oxlint clean.

---

> **Update 2026-06-28 — head `228ade8544e`: rebased the full stack onto current `main`.** Conflict resolved (`scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.mjs` surface budgets reconciled to main's current values + this stack's deltas; `config-types` deprecated budget 416→417 for the new public `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns` type). The plugin-compat [P1] fix (source-aware host-FS default-deny) survives the rebase and is **re-proven against current main**: in the `node:22-bookworm-slim` arm64 container, reverting only the resolver fix makes the two plugin-compat tests fail with `expected 404 to be 200`, and restoring it passes — so the regression exists on current main and the fix removes it. Full gate green on the rebased head: `tools-invoke-http.test.ts` 176/176, `audit-gateway-tools-http.test.ts` 19/19, `schema.help.quality.test.ts` 23/23, `plugin-sdk-surface-report --check` clean, oxlint clean, typecheck clean. Now `MERGEABLE` against main. The host-FS write boundary still needs explicit maintainer/security acceptance (a product decision, not an author fix).

---

> **Update 2026-06-28 — head `acb9ab0a39d`: fix ClawSweeper [P1] plugin-name compatibility.** Adding `read`/`write`/`edit` to `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY` name-denied any *same-named plugin tool* over `/tools/invoke` + SDK `tools.invoke`, so a pre-existing non-optional plugin `read`/`write`/`edit` returned 404 even with the host-FS opt-ins off. Per the resolver, the default-deny is now **source-aware** (`src/security/dangerous-tools.ts` `HOST_FS_BUILTIN_CODING_DENY_NAMES` + the gateway deny filter in `src/gateway/tool-resolution.ts`): a same-named tool whose *only* deny reason is the host-FS built-in coding default is preserved when it carries plugin metadata (`getPluginToolMeta`); the built-in coding tool (no plugin meta) stays gated behind the owner gate + opt-in, and owner-only / explicit `gateway.tools.deny` / `exec`/`fs_write`/etc. are unaffected. Two regression tests assert a plugin `read`/`write` stays reachable **without** a new `gateway.tools.allow` entry. Verified in the `node:22-bookworm-slim` arm64 container: `tools-invoke-http.test.ts` 176/176, `audit-gateway-tools-http.test.ts` 19/19, `schema.help.quality.test.ts` 23/23; oxlint clean. Red/green proof: reverting only the resolver fix makes both new tests fail with `expected 404 to be 200`. **Still pending: rebase onto current `main`** (main refactored the gateway deny-handling path, so the rebase needs careful re-expression — tracked separately).

---

> **Update 2026-06-17 — head `4fe6f873c9` (scope owner gate to built-in host-FS write tools; restacked on #85664 `d625c6de23`).** Mirrors #85664's read-side fix for `write`/`edit` and addresses the ClawSweeper plugin-fallback finding (`docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md` write section). Restacked onto #85664's new base `d625c6de23` (which carries the read-side scoping); the write-side delta on top scopes the overstated "refused even when both config keys are set" wording to the **built-in** `write`/`edit` tools across the `hostFsWrite` config help (`src/config/schema.help.ts`), the `hostFsWrite` member comment (`src/config/types.gateway.ts`), the `hostFsWrite` bullet in `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md`, and the write section of `docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md` (prose, truth table, _Plugin name collision_ note). Per the resolver (`src/gateway/tool-resolution.ts`): for a non-owner caller the built-in is never materialized, but a same-named allowlisted plugin tool still resolves under the normal `gateway.tools.allow`/`deny` policy. Help strings / type comments / docs only — **no runtime code changed** (verified: the write-side commit `27deceb882..4fe6f873c9` touches only `src/config/schema.help.ts`, `src/config/types.gateway.ts`, and the two `docs/gateway/*.md` files — no `src/gateway/`, `src/security/`, `zod-schema.ts`, or `schema.labels.ts`), so the nine-case live proof below stands unchanged. Verified in the `node:22-bookworm-slim` linux container: `pnpm check:docs` (format clean, markdownlint 0 errors, MDX ok, 0 broken links) and `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts` (23/23) both green.

---

> **Update 2026-06-17 — head `61e6f29bed` (config-help owner-gate fix; resolves [P2]/[P3]).** Restacked onto #85664's new base `7f9876c2b2`. The only delta vs the last-reviewed head `661d6838c0` is **config help / type docs** (verified: `git diff 661d6838c0 61e6f29bed` touches only `src/config/schema.help.ts` +4 and `src/config/types.gateway.ts` +25 — comment/string lines; every runtime file under `src/gateway/`, `src/security/`, `zod-schema.ts`, `schema.labels.ts` is byte-identical): the `hostFsWrite` `FIELD_HELP` string and the `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns` summary + `hostFsWrite` member comment now document the **owner/admin sender** gate (`senderIsOwner === true`) the resolver enforces, and the type summary is refreshed so it no longer calls `hostFsWrite` "future work". This directly resolves ClawSweeper **[P2]** (config help omits the owner gate) and **[P3]** (stale `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns` summary). Because no runtime code changed, the nine-case live proof below stands unchanged. Verified in the `node:22-bookworm-slim` linux container: `schema.help.quality.test.ts` (23/23) and `pnpm check:docs` both green.

---

> **Update 2026-06-17 — head `661d6838c0` (docs owner-gate fix; resolves [P2]).** Restacked onto #85664's docs-updated read base `ca46eb9946`. The only delta vs the last-reviewed head `afe36ccf45` is **docs**: the `read` + `write`/`edit` truth tables in `docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md` and the `hostFsRead`/`hostFsWrite` bullets in `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md` now document the **owner/admin sender** gate (`senderIsOwner === true`) the resolver enforces — directly resolving ClawSweeper's **[P2]** ("Document the owner gate for file-tool opt-ins"). No source files changed (verified: `git diff afe36ccf45 661d6838c0` touches only those two docs files), so `dist/` is byte-identical and the nine-case live proof below stands unchanged. `pnpm check:docs` passes in the `node:22-bookworm-slim` linux container (format clean, markdownlint 0 errors, MDX ok, 0 broken links).

---

Builds on #85664. Narrow extension of the broader direct-invoke umbrella tracked in #37131.

## Summary

Extends #85664's narrow read-only landing to also expose `write` and `edit` over the gateway direct-invoke surfaces (HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` AND SDK RPC `tools.invoke`, which share the `resolveGatewayScopedTools` resolver). Same **triple-key owner-gated** shape as #85664's `read`: a surface-specific class opt-in **plus** the per-tool name in `gateway.tools.allow` **plus** `senderIsOwner === true`, all on the `http` surface.

## Re-stacked on #85664's owner-gated read base (head `afe36ccf456f`, base upstream `023ce6e96c38`)

This branch was rebuilt on top of #85664's current head `5ca72af1ba` (itself rebased onto current upstream `023ce6e96c38`) and the write/edit extension re-applied with the **same owner-gate**. This directly resolves the prior ClawSweeper [P1].

**Bot [P1] (resolved):** upstream commit `02182d5a3031` ("refactor: remove sender owner tool gating") deleted the `ownerOnly` enforcement helper, so the earlier `ownerOnly: true` metadata tag this PR added became dead code — a trusted-proxy `operator.write` caller could have reached an allowlisted mutating tool with no owner check. **Fix:** this PR no longer relies on that tag. `write`/`edit` are materialized into the direct-invoke candidate set **only when the resolved sender is owner** (`senderIsOwner === true`, evaluated at resolve time in `tool-resolution.ts`), exactly mirroring #85664's read gate. A trusted-proxy `operator.write` caller is now refused even with both config keys set (proven below, cases 2 & 4).

## Triple-key gating

To reach a write-class tool on the direct-invoke surface, ALL must hold:

| Key | Purpose | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `gateway.tools.directInvoke.hostFsWrite: true` | Class-level opt-in for the whole write family. | `false` |
| `gateway.tools.allow: [...]` includes the tool name | Per-tool enablement. | not present |
| `senderIsOwner === true` | Caller resolves to owner/admin (trusted-proxy `operator.admin`, or shared-secret bearer). | — |

Truth table (tool name `T` ∈ `{write, edit}`, surface = `http`):

| `allow` ∋ `T` | `hostFsWrite` | sender owner | `T` reachable |
|---|---|---|---|
| no  | no  | —   | ❌ |
| yes | no  | yes | ❌ (class opt-in off; built-in not materialized; `dangerous_allow` audit fires) |
| no  | yes | yes | ❌ (not in allow) |
| yes | yes | **no**  | ❌ (**owner gate** — refused) |
| yes | yes | yes | ✅ |

## Scope

- **Adds:** `write`, `edit` to direct-invoke (behind `hostFsWrite: true` + allow + owner).
- **Inherits from #85664:** `read` via `hostFsRead: true` + allow + owner.
- **`apply_patch`:** present in `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY` for defense-in-depth, but the coding-tools factory produces no `apply_patch` entry, so it is never materialized (proven below, case 7).
- **NOT exposed:** `exec`, `process`, `spawn`, `shell` — RCE-class; out of scope for this PR.

## Audit

- `gateway.tools_invoke_http.host_write_allow` fires only when BOTH `hostFsWrite: true` AND a write-class name is in `allow` (mirrors `host_read_allow`); escalates to `critical` on non-loopback bind.
- `dangerous_allow` suppression for `write`/`edit` is **source-aware** — suppressed only when the `hostFsWrite` class opt-in is active; `apply_patch` is never suppressed.

## Files changed

- `src/config/types.gateway.ts` — adds `hostFsWrite?: boolean` to `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns`
- `src/config/zod-schema.ts` — `hostFsWrite` schema entry
- `src/config/schema.help.ts`, `src/config/schema.labels.ts` — field help + label
- `src/security/dangerous-tools.ts` — adds `"write"`, `"edit"` to `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY`
- `src/gateway/tool-resolution.ts` — owner-gated write/edit materialization; coding built-ins win plugin name collision when class opt-in active
- `src/security/audit-gateway-config.ts` — `host_write_allow` finding; source-aware `dangerous_allow` suppression
- `src/gateway/tools-invoke-http.test.ts` — write coding-tool collision precedence (owner→built-in, non-owner→plugin, opt-in-off→plugin)
- `src/security/audit-gateway-tools-http.test.ts` — `host_write_allow` dual-key + source-aware `dangerous_allow` suppression cases (incl. `apply_patch` never suppressed)
- `src/agents/agent-tools.ts` — coding-tool factory plumbing
- `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md`, `docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md`, `docs/gateway/security/audit-checks.md` — `hostFsWrite` opt-in, triple-key truth tables, `host_write_allow` catalog row

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Extend #85664's owner-gated direct-invoke `read` to also expose `write` and `edit` over the Gateway HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` and SDK `tools.invoke` surfaces, behind the same triple key (class opt-in `directInvoke.hostFsWrite` + per-tool `gateway.tools.allow` + `senderIsOwner`). Re-stacked on #85664's owner-gated read base so write/edit materialize only for an owner sender — resolving the prior [P1] where upstream `02182d5a3031` made the old `ownerOnly` tag dead code.

**Real environment tested:** Built fresh from head `afe36ccf456f` inside a `node:22-bookworm-slim` arm64-native container (full runtime build → `dist/index.js`; SDK → `packages/sdk/dist/index.mjs`). Each case boots a live `openclaw gateway run` on a real loopback socket; owner = trusted-proxy `operator.admin` (and a shared-secret bearer token for the SDK case), non-owner = trusted-proxy `operator.write`. Target file states are re-read from disk after each call to prove real host mutation or its absence.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:** `PROOF_HEAD=afe36ccf456f bash .proof-63919.sh` inside the container — boots nine gateway configs and dispatches `read`/`write`/`edit`/`apply_patch` over HTTP `/tools/invoke` and SDK `tools.invoke`, asserting the HTTP status and re-reading each target file from disk.

**Evidence after fix:** live terminal output captured from the container at head `afe36ccf456f`:

```
=== HEAD: afe36ccf456f ===

### LIVE PROOF (HTTP trusted-proxy surface) ###

--- 1 W-OWNER-ENABLED (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 200 {"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Successfully wrote 24 bytes to /work/.proof63/w1.txt"}]}}
  file=OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f
--- 2 W-NONOWNER-DENIED (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=true, operator.write, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.write] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}
  file=PRESEED
--- 3 E-OWNER-ENABLED (allow=[edit] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=edit) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 200 {"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Successfully replaced 1 block(s) in /work/.proof63/e3.txt."}],"details":{"diff":" 1 ALPHA\\n-2 BETA\\n+2 GAMMA-afe36ccf456f", ...}}}
  file=ALPHA / GAMMA-afe36ccf456f
--- 4 E-NONOWNER-DENIED (allow=[edit] hostFsWrite=true, operator.write, tool=edit) ---
  [HTTP operator.write] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: edit"}}
  file=ALPHA / BETA  (unchanged)
--- 5 W-DENIED-no-opt-in (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=false, operator.admin, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}
  file=<absent>
--- 6 W-DENIED-no-allow (allow=[] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}
  file=<absent>
--- 7 APPLYPATCH-denied (allow=[apply_patch] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=apply_patch) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: apply_patch"}}
--- 8 READ-REGRESSION (allow=[read] hostFsRead=true, operator.admin, tool=read) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 200 {"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"READ-BASE-OK-afe36ccf456f\\n"}]}}

### LIVE PROOF (SDK token surface — shared-secret bearer = owner) ###

--- 9 SDK-W-OWNER (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=true, token bearer, tool=write) ---
  [SDK token] {"ok":true,"toolName":"write","output":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Successfully wrote 28 bytes to /work/.proof63/w9.txt"}]},"source":"core"}
  file=SDK-OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f

=== PROOF COMPLETE: HEAD=afe36ccf456f ===
```

Independent re-read of persisted file states after the run: `w1.txt`=`OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f`, `w2.txt`=`PRESEED` (non-owner write left it untouched), `e3.txt`=`ALPHA/GAMMA-afe36ccf456f`, `e4.txt`=`ALPHA/BETA` (non-owner edit left it untouched), `w5.txt` and `w6.txt` absent (never created), `w9.txt`=`SDK-OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f`.

**Observed result after fix:** All nine cases behaved as specified — owner writes/edits land and mutate the host file; a non-owner `operator.write` caller is refused `write`/`edit` (404) with the target file left untouched; missing class opt-in or missing allow entry both deny; `apply_patch` is never materialized; and the inherited `read` path still works:

| # | Case | Config | Caller | HTTP/SDK | File state |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | write owner | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.admin` | 200 wrote 24 bytes | written |
| 2 | write **non-owner** | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.write` | 404 not_found | **unchanged** (owner gate) |
| 3 | edit owner | allow=[edit], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.admin` | 200 replaced 1 block | edited |
| 4 | edit **non-owner** | allow=[edit], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.write` | 404 not_found | **unchanged** (owner gate) |
| 5 | write no opt-in | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=false | `operator.admin` | 404 not_found | never created |
| 6 | write no allow | allow=[], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.admin` | 404 not_found | never created |
| 7 | apply_patch | allow=[apply_patch], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.admin` | 404 not_found | factory never materializes it |
| 8 | read regression | allow=[read], hostFsRead=true | `operator.admin` | 200 + contents | #85664 base intact |
| 9 | write owner (SDK) | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=true | token bearer | ok=true wrote 28 bytes | written |

Supplemental (not a substitute for the live proof above), all in the same `node:22-bookworm-slim` arm64 container at head `afe36ccf456f`: unit tests — **160 passed** across the gateway test projects (`tools-invoke-http` 42 + `tool-resolution` 5 + `audit-gateway-tools-http` 19, ×3 projects = 7 file-projects); `lint` (oxlint, 0/0), `tsgo:core` (0), `check:test-types` (0), and `plugin-sdk-surface-report` vitest (4/4). **`checks-node-core-fast` / plugin-SDK surface budget:** this PR adds `hostFsWrite?` as a *field* on the existing `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns` type — **zero new top-level exports** — so `plugin-sdk-surface-report --check` passes on the `+1` budget already carried by #85664's base commit (`config-types` 416 / public 10285 / public-fn 5164 / deprecated 3238). No further budget change in this PR.

**Not tested:** `exec`, `process`, `spawn`, `shell` (RCE-class; out of scope for this PR and never exposed by the direct-invoke factory).

commands.review_summary:

commands.author: eleqtrizit

target.title: fix(memory): require privileged dreaming config changes
target.body:
## Summary

Tightens Memory Core `/dreaming on|off` so persistent dreaming configuration changes require a privileged caller.

## Changes

- Opts the bundled Memory Core `/dreaming` command into trusted owner-status exposure.
- Requires owner status for channel callers, or `operator.admin` for gateway clients, before mutating dreaming config.
- Keeps `/dreaming status` and help/phase output readable without mutating config.
- Adds regression coverage for non-owner denial, owner success, gateway write-scope denial, gateway admin success, and command registration owner exposure.

## Validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-command.test.ts extensions/memory-core/index.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`

## Notes

Related: NVIDIA-dev/openclaw-tracking#788

No `CHANGELOG.md` changes.
target.author: eleqtrizit

target.title: fix(pdf): guard native provider requests
target.body:
## Summary

Route native Anthropic/Gemini PDF provider requests through OpenClaw's shared guarded provider HTTP path.


## Changes

- Use `resolveProviderHttpRequestConfig` and `postJsonRequest` for native PDF provider POSTs.
- Thread resolved model headers and provider request transport metadata from the PDF tool into native PDF helpers.
- Replace successful provider `res.json()` parsing with bounded `readProviderJsonResponse` parsing.
- Add focused regressions for private-network blocking, explicit `allowPrivateNetwork` opt-in, and oversized successful JSON responses.

## Validation

- `corepack pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/pdf-native-providers.test.ts src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm format:check -- src/agents/tools/pdf-native-providers.ts src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.ts src/agents/tools/pdf-native-providers.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:core`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local --parallel-tests "node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/pdf-native-providers.test.ts src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.test.ts"` clean, no accepted/actionable findings

## Notes

- AI-assisted.
- No `CHANGELOG.md` update.
- `corepack pnpm check:changed -- src/agents/tools/pdf-native-providers.ts src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.ts src/agents/tools/pdf-native-providers.test.ts` did not run checks because the delegated Blacksmith Testbox path failed at local `crabbox` binary sanity checks before executing the lane.

target.title: fix(voice): require admin for voice set
target.body:
## Summary

Require admin or explicit owner authority before Talk Voice persists a new voice selection.

## Changes

- Gate the mutating voice set command on operator.admin when gateway scopes are present.
- Expose and honor owner status for absent-scope channel command contexts.
- Cover external-channel denial, owner/admin success, and read-only command behavior in Talk Voice tests.

## Validation

- corepack pnpm test extensions/talk-voice/index.test.ts
- git diff --check -- extensions/talk-voice/index.ts extensions/talk-voice/index.test.ts
- .agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local

## Notes

- AI-assisted.
- No CHANGELOG.md update; release notes are maintainer-owned.
- USER.md contains local worklog only and is not committed.

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where authorized non-owner channel command senders could persist a gateway-wide Talk voice change when no gateway scope metadata was present.

## Why This Change Was Made

Persistent Talk voice selection is a gateway configuration write, so the mutating command now requires the same admin or owner authority used by adjacent config-writing command handlers. Read-only voice status and list commands remain available.

## User Impact

Operators get consistent admin-only behavior for persistent Talk voice changes. Non-owner command senders can still inspect voice status or list voices when otherwise authorized, but cannot persist a new voice selection.

## Evidence

The focused Talk Voice test file passes with 17 tests, including external Telegram/Discord denial without admin, admin success, owner success, and read-only command coverage.

target.title: fix(gateway): log terminal persistence failures in chat abort cleanup
target.body:
Fixes #97795

## Summary

Fix silent swallowing of terminal-state persistence failures across three Gateway boundaries:

1. `src/gateway/chat-abort.ts` — cleanup catch block
2. `src/gateway/server-runtime-subscriptions.ts` — delayed cleanup ordering (persistence attached after `cleanup()` was requested)
3. `src/gateway/server-chat.ts` — owner-side terminal lifecycle persistence fallback catch

## Real Behavior Proof

Environment: Linux, Node v22.23.1, pnpm 11.2.2, worktree SHA 1052652a71

Before fix (new regression test on old code):
```bash
$ pnpm test src/gateway/chat-abort.test.ts
AssertionError: expected "vi.fn()" to be called once, but got 0 times
Tests  4 failed | 124 passed (128)
```

After fix:
```bash
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/chat-abort.test.ts src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts
Test Files  6 passed (6)
     Tests  326 passed (326)
```

Real behavior proof (chat-abort cleanup path):
```bash
$ tsx /tmp/proof-97795.mjs
[PROOF LOGGER] Failed to persist chat abort terminal state for run proof-run-1: simulated persistence failure {"error":{}}
Controllers remaining after cleanup: 0
```

The script creates a `registerChatAbortController` registration with a real `log.error` logger, attaches a rejecting `projectSessionTerminalPersistence` promise, calls `cleanup()`, and observes the diagnostic log plus correct controller removal.

Lint / Format checks (changed files):
```bash
$ oxlint src/gateway/chat-abort.ts src/gateway/chat-abort.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts src/gateway/server-chat.ts src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts src/gateway/server-runtime-subscriptions.ts src/gateway/server.impl.ts
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.

$ oxfmt --check ...
All matched files use the correct format.
```

## Regression Test Plan

- `src/gateway/chat-abort.test.ts`
  - Added `logs an error and still removes the registration when terminal persistence fails`
- `src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts`
  - Updated `broadcasts a terminal fallback snapshot when persistence fails` to assert `log.error` is called

## Root Cause

Three separate `.catch(() => { ... })` handlers discarded the rejection reason:
- `src/gateway/chat-abort.ts:176`
- `src/gateway/server-runtime-subscriptions.ts:100`
- `src/gateway/server-chat.ts:714`

## Implementation Plan Details

1. Add optional `log?: SubsystemLogger` to `registerChatAbortController`;
2. Add optional `log?: SubsystemLogger` to `startGatewayEventSubscriptions` and pass it to `createAgentEventHandler`;
3. Add optional `log?: SubsystemLogger` to `createAgentEventHandler`;
4. Pass `context.logGateway` from `agent.ts` / `chat.ts` to `registerChatAbortController`;
5. Pass `log` from `server.impl.ts` to `startGatewayEventSubscriptions`;
6. Call `log?.error(...)` in all three catch blocks, using `formatErrorMessage(err)`;
7. Add/update regression tests;
8. Demonstrate real log output for the chat-abort path with a standalone script.

## Change Details

- `src/gateway/chat-abort.ts`
  - Import `formatErrorMessage`, `SubsystemLogger`
  - `registerChatAbortController` params gain `log?: SubsystemLogger`
  - Cleanup catch now logs the error

- `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.ts` / `chat.ts`
  - `registerChatAbortController(...)` calls add `log: context.logGateway`

- `src/gateway/server-chat.ts`
  - Import `formatErrorMessage`, `SubsystemLogger`
  - `AgentEventHandlerOptions` gains `log?: SubsystemLogger`
  - `createAgentEventHandler` destructures `log`
  - Terminal lifecycle persistence catch now logs the error

- `src/gateway/server-runtime-subscriptions.ts`
  - Import `formatErrorMessage`, `SubsystemLogger`
  - `startGatewayEventSubscriptions` params gain `log?: SubsystemLogger`
  - Delayed cleanup catch now logs the error
  - Pass `log` to `createAgentEventHandler`

- `src/gateway/server.impl.ts`
  - `startGatewayEventSubscriptions(...)` call adds `log`

- `src/gateway/chat-abort.test.ts`
  - New regression test

- `src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts`
  - `createHarness` creates a mock logger and passes it to `createAgentEventHandler`
  - Updated persistence-failure test asserts `log.error` is called

## Test Points

- chat-abort new test fails before fix (RED) and passes after fix (GREEN)
- server-chat existing persistence-failure test now asserts `log.error` and passes
- All 326 relevant tests pass
- Changed files pass oxlint / oxfmt
- `pnpm tsgo:core`, `pnpm tsgo:core:test`, `pnpm check:import-cycles` pass

## Next Steps

1. Commit the updated changes;
2. Force-push the updated branch (amended commit);
3. Paste the updated PR body;
4. Comment `@clawsweeper re-review`.

commands.author: cxbAsDev

target.title: fix(matrix): bound raw transport response reads to prevent OOM
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Matrix raw transport responses with omitted `maxBytes` called `response.arrayBuffer()`, buffering the entire response body without any size guard. This is an OOM vector against untrusted Matrix homeservers. Additionally, encrypted media download size limits were silently dropped at the crypto facade boundary, and the expensive native crypto binding load ran before the download could fail on oversized media.

## Why This Change Was Made

Three related fixes in the Matrix transport/crypto chain:

1. **`transport.ts`**: Always bounds raw response reads with a default cap (reuses the existing `MATRIX_SDK_RESPONSE_MAX_BYTES` = 64 MiB). The previously-unbounded `response.arrayBuffer()` fallback is removed entirely.
2. **`sdk.ts`**: Forwards `opts` through the crypto facade download callback into `this.downloadContent(mxcUrl, opts)` instead of dropping them.
3. **`crypto-facade.ts`**: Reorders `deps.downloadContent(file.url, opts)` before `loadMatrixCryptoNodeBindings()`, so oversized encrypted media fails fast.

## Evidence

**Environment**: Linux, Node 22, OpenClaw main

### Transport tests — 15/15 pass, stream cancellation proved

```
$ pnpm test extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/transport.test.ts --run --reporter=verbose

 ✓ rejects oversized raw responses before buffering the whole body 31ms
 ✓ rejects malformed raw content-length before buffering the body 5ms
 ✓ applies streaming byte limits when raw responses omit content-length 16ms
 ✓ uses the matrix-specific idle-timeout error for stalled raw downloads 39ms
 ✓ uses undici runtime fetch for pinned Matrix requests 5ms
 ✓ rejects oversized JSON responses via content-length before buffering the body 2ms
 ✓ applies streaming byte limits when JSON responses omit content-length 2ms
 ✓ uses the JSON-specific idle-timeout error for stalled JSON downloads 4ms
 ✓ returns full JSON bodies that stay under the byte limit 2ms
 ✓ rejects oversized raw responses via content-length when maxBytes is omitted (default SDK cap) 2ms
 ✓ allows small raw buffer bodies under the default SDK cap when maxBytes is omitted 2ms
 ✓ respects explicit maxBytes over the default cap for raw responses 1ms
 ✓ rejects and cancels SDK responses above the declared size limit 2ms
 ✓ strips matrix-js-sdk state_after sync opt-in from /sync requests 3ms
 ✓ leaves non-sync Matrix requests unchanged 1ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  15 passed (15)
```

**Key assertions in oversized tests**: `expect(cancel).toHaveBeenCalledOnce()` — proves the ReadableStream is cancelled by the bounded reader at the cap, not fully buffered.

### Crypto facade options-forwarding

```
$ pnpm test extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk.test.ts --run --reporter=verbose -t "forwards encrypted media"

 ✓ forwards encrypted media download options through the crypto facade 2ms
```

### Full Matrix suite: 123 files, 1420 tests, 0 failures

## Risk

Low. The 64 MiB default cap reuses the existing `MATRIX_SDK_RESPONSE_MAX_BYTES` constant already applied in `createMatrixGuardedFetch`. Explicit `maxBytes` values continue to work unchanged.

target.title: fix(gateway): log errors when terminal persistence rejects in chat-abort cleanup
target.body:
Related to #97795.

## What Problem This Solves

`persistGatewaySessionLifecycleEvent` failures in `createAgentEventHandler` are silently swallowed — the terminal lifecycle handler catches rejection only to broadcast a fallback session snapshot, without logging the failure. Operators cannot observe session-store write failures in production.

## Why This Change Was Made

Log the rejected persistence at the **owner boundary** (`server-chat.ts`) where the promise is created and its rejection is already caught for fallback projection. This is the single correct site — it owns both the persistence promise and the fallback projection. Logging in cleanup consumers (`chat-abort.ts`, `server-runtime-subscriptions.ts`) would be downstream duplicates that miss the owner/fallback case and risk double-reporting.

A module-level `createSubsystemLogger("gateway/server-chat")` follows the established gateway pattern.

## Root Cause

`introduced by` commit `7c47904bb42d` (joshavant). The original owner catch only broadcast a fallback session snapshot; it never logged the write failure.

Confidence: **clear** — the `.catch(() => { broadcastSessionChange(evt); })` handler visibly omits error recording.

## User Impact

None — this is an observability fix for operators.

## Evidence

**Behavior addressed**: When `persistGatewaySessionLifecycleEvent` rejects, the gateway logs the failure with the run ID and error before broadcasting the fallback session snapshot. Logging happens once at the persistence owner boundary.

**Real environment tested**: Linux (kernel 4.19), Node v22, branch `fix/chat-abort-persistence-log-97795` (HEAD `803e4d5499`), based on origin/main `68ead4dd80`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```console
$ node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/gateway/server-chat.ts src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts
EXIT=0

$ npx oxfmt --check src/gateway/server-chat.ts src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.
```

**Evidence after fix**:

| Persistence outcome | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| `persistGatewaySessionLifecycleEvent` resolves | `markPersisted()` + broadcast (existing) | unchanged |
| `persistGatewaySessionLifecycleEvent` rejects | silent fallback broadcast only | **`log.error`** → then fallback broadcast |

**Test output** — assertion added to existing fallback test:

```console
$ npx vitest run src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts --reporter=verbose

 ✓ |gateway-server| agent event handler > broadcasts a terminal fallback snapshot when persistence fails  5ms
 ✓ |gateway-server| agent event handler > does not clear a same-id retry when an old restart terminal arrives  3ms
 ✓ |gateway-server| agent event handler > ...

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  95 passed (95)
   Duration  10.24s
```

The existing test "broadcasts a terminal fallback snapshot when persistence fails" now additionally asserts `mockLogError` was called with `"gateway: failed to persist terminal session lifecycle event for run run-failed-write: Error: disk full"`.

**Observed result after fix**: When `persistGatewaySessionLifecycleEventMock` rejects with `new Error("disk full")`, the owner catch logs the error via `log.error` and still broadcasts the fallback `sessions.changed` snapshot. The `markTrackedRunTerminalPersisted` callback is still not called (persistence failed). All 95 existing tests pass.

**What was not tested**:
- Forcing an actual disk write failure in a live gateway session store — the vitest suite exercises the real `createAgentEventHandler` production code and asserts the log is emitted from the exact same `.catch()` block that the compiled gateway runs

## Regression Test Plan

```console
$ npx vitest run src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts --reporter=verbose

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  95 passed (95)
```

Modified test `broadcasts a terminal fallback snapshot when persistence fails`:
1. `persistGatewaySessionLifecycleEventMock` rejects with `new Error("disk full")`
2. Send terminal lifecycle event via `handler({...})`
3. Assert fallback `sessions.changed` broadcast (existing)
4. Assert `mockLogError` called with message containing run ID and error text **(new)**

## Changes

- `src/gateway/server-chat.ts`: +8/−1 lines — `createSubsystemLogger` import, module-level logger, `log.error` in the persistence owner `.catch()`
- `src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts`: +11 lines — `vi.mock` for logging subsystem, `mockLogError` assertion in the existing fallback snapshot test

commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: fix(context-engine): bound deferred turn maintenance with a per-task timeout (AI-assisted)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

A post-turn context-engine **deferred turn maintenance** run can wedge (for example on plugin lock contention) with **no per-task timeout below the ~360s session-level abort**. While it is wedged it holds its maintenance lane, so a user message queued behind it on the same session can wait minutes before being processed, even though the model is healthy. `session.stalled` also re-warns on every ~30s heartbeat with no backoff, spamming the logs.

This matches the report in #96703: an interactive session effectively hangs until a manual stop.

## Why This Change Was Made

Three concrete gaps in core code (independent of any specific context-engine plugin):

1. **No per-task timeout was available on the maintenance enqueue.** `scheduleDeferredTurnMaintenance` enqueued the maintenance worker via `enqueueCommandInLane(...)` with no options. The command queue already supports a per-task timeout (`taskTimeoutMs` → rejects with `CommandLaneTaskTimeoutError` and releases the lane), but the maintenance lane never wired it up, so an operator hitting the wedge had no knob below the session-level abort.
2. **A timed-out maintenance worker was not fenced.** Rejecting the enqueue promise releases the lane, but it does not stop the still-running worker. A wedged worker could later call `runtimeContext.rewriteTranscriptEntries()` or complete/fail the task **after** the active run was cleared and a queued foreground turn proceeded, breaking the same-session wait-before-read invariant.
3. **`session.stalled` had no log backoff.** `session.stuck` and `session.long_running` already throttle repeats; `session.stalled` did not.

This change adds the bound + fence mechanism and ships it **opt-in / disabled by default**, so existing deployments whose background maintenance legitimately runs longer than a couple of minutes are not force-cancelled on upgrade. An operator hitting the wedge sets the new key to a positive millisecond value to arm it.

## User Impact

- **No behavior change on upgrade unless configured.** The per-task timeout is **disabled by default** — background turn maintenance keeps running unbounded exactly as before. There is no new force-cancel of long-running background engines on upgrade.
- Operators experiencing the wedge **opt in** by setting `agents.defaults.compaction.turnMaintenanceTaskTimeoutMs` to a positive millisecond value (absent or `0` keeps it disabled). When set, a wedged run is bounded at that value and the lane is released, so a queued user message proceeds at ~the timeout instead of waiting for the session-level abort.
- When the bound is armed and fires, late maintenance side effects are **fenced**: the runtime-owned `rewriteTranscriptEntries` wrapper and the worker's terminal complete/fail/descriptor writes become no-ops, so a non-cooperative plugin cannot corrupt session state after the foreground turn has resumed. When the bound is disabled, the fence is never armed.
- A persistently stalled session no longer floods the logs; the first `session.stalled` warning is immediate, repeats are throttled, and recovery is never gated by the backoff.

### Changes

- `command-queue`: add an optional `onTaskTimeout` hook fired synchronously when the per-task timeout rejects, before the lane is released.
- `context-engine-maintenance`: resolve an **opt-in** per-task timeout from `agents.defaults.compaction.turnMaintenanceTaskTimeoutMs` (absent / `0` / non-positive ⇒ no bound, no fence); when a positive value is set, pass `taskTimeoutMs` + `onTaskTimeout` to the deferred maintenance enqueue and trip a per-run fence on timeout (hook + defensive trip in the timeout catch); thread the fence into the worker and the runtime-context `rewriteTranscriptEntries` wrapper (checked at entry, inside the session-manager rewrite lock, and before the runtime-transcript write) and before the worker's `completeTaskRunByRunId` / `failTaskRunByRunId` / progress writes.
- `context-engine-maintenance`: scope the timeout classification to the resolved maintenance lane — only this deferred lane's `CommandLaneTaskTimeoutError` trips the timeout/fence path; a foreign-lane queue timeout surfacing on the enqueue falls through to the normal schedule-failure path (the lane is computed once and reused for enqueue + classification so they cannot drift).
- `session.stalled`: apply the existing `session.stuck`/`session.long_running` warn backoff (`max(last + threshold, last * 2)`), first emission immediate, recovery never gated.
- Config schema accepts `0` (`nonnegative`) as the explicit "disable" sentinel; help/types/docs document the opt-in default.
- New unit coverage: queue `onTaskTimeout` hook; timeout disabled by default and when set to `0`; configured bound + fence arming; late rewrite fenced after timeout; timed-out task not resurrected to succeeded/failed; checkpoint-after-timeout ordering; `session.stalled` backoff.

## Evidence

AI-assisted PR (implemented with an AI coding agent; author reviewed and understands the change).

**Static / unit validation** (this branch):
- `tsgo:core`, `tsgo:test:src` — clean
- `command-queue.test.ts` — 34 passed; `context-engine-maintenance.test.ts` — 60 passed (incl. disabled-by-default, `0`-disables, and fence cases)
- `oxfmt --check` + oxlint on touched files — clean
- `CHANGELOG.md` not touched (left for maintainers at landing)

**Real runtime reproduction (end-to-end, in a container).** A real gateway was started in a container running this branch, with a real context-engine plugin selected via `plugins.slots.contextEngine` whose `info.turnMaintenanceMode = "background"` (so a normal `reason:"turn"` run is deferred through `scheduleDeferredTurnMaintenance`). Its `maintain()` deliberately sleeps 12s (longer than the configured timeout) and then attempts a late transcript rewrite. The bound was **explicitly opted in** for the test: `agents.defaults.compaction.turnMaintenanceTaskTimeoutMs = 5000`. Two real agent turns were run on one session. Everything in the queue/timeout/fence/transcript-rewrite path is real production code; only the plugin's deliberate sleep is contrived. Logs redacted (provider/model/host/ids replaced with placeholders):

```
[T+0.00s] [context-engine] deferred turn maintenance queued taskId=<id-1> sessionKey=<session> lane=context-engine-turn-maintenance:<session>
[T+0.00s] [wedge] maintain#1 START                       # deferred maintenance begins, will sleep 12s
[T+2.87s] (queued) second turn is waiting behind the in-flight deferred maintenance on the session lane
[T+5.00s] [diagnostic] lane task error: lane=context-engine-turn-maintenance:<session> durationMs=5000 error="CommandLaneTaskTimeoutError: ... task timed out after 5000ms"
[T+5.00s] [agent/embedded] deferred context engine maintenance timed out: sessionKey=<session> taskId=<id-1> taskTimeoutMs=5000
[T+5.06s] [agent/embedded] embedded run start: <turn-2>   # queued turn RELEASED at the 5s timeout — NOT after the 12s wedge, NOT after the ~360s session abort
[T+12.00s] [wedge] maintain#1 WOKE after 12000ms sleep, attempting late rewrite   # the wedged worker finally wakes, 7s after the lane was released
[T+12.01s] [wedge] maintain#1 late rewrite result: {"changed":false,"bytesFreed":0,"rewrittenEntries":0,"reason":"maintenance fenced after timeout"}   # FENCED -> no-op
```

Fence scope is correct: the second turn's **foreground** maintenance (not timed out) is **not** fenced (`reason: "no replacements requested"`); only the timed-out **deferred** runs are fenced. With the bound left at its default (disabled), neither the timeout nor the fence arms and background maintenance runs unbounded as before. A scripted reproduction is available on request.

Closes #96703

commands.author: zw-xysk

target.title: fix(proxy): apply enhanced NO_PROXY matching to global undici dispatcher (fixes #95998)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #95998.

When `HTTPS_PROXY` is set, the global EnvHttpProxyAgent routes ALL requests through the proxy — including internal plugin requests that should bypass via `NO_PROXY`. The built-in undici NO_PROXY matcher does not support leading-dot subdomain patterns, CIDR notation, or octet wildcards.

OpenClaw already has `matchesNoProxy()` that handles these — but it was only used by provider helpers, not the global dispatcher.

## Changes Made

1. **NO_PROXY-aware proxy dispatcher** — Wraps every EnvHttpProxyAgent with a Proxy that checks `matchesNoProxy()` before dispatching. Bypassed requests are routed through a direct Agent with the same timeout and connect policy.

2. **Lifecycle synchronization** — `close()` and `destroy()` now await both the proxy dispatcher AND the bypass Agent before firing the completion callback (addresses Codex P1 finding).

3. **Timeout/connect consistency** — `forceResetGlobalDispatcher` now passes `connect` options to the bypass agent, matching `ensureGlobalUndiciEnvProxyDispatcher` and `applyGlobalDispatcherStreamTimeouts` (addresses Codex P2 finding).

## Evidence

**Proof from unit tests (35 tests):**
```
$ npx vitest run src/infra/net/undici-global-dispatcher.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  35 passed (35)
```

**Real proxy behavior proof:**

A local HTTP proxy (127.0.0.1:18999) was started and `HTTPS_PROXY`/`HTTP_PROXY` configured to point to it. The global dispatcher was initialized via `ensureGlobalUndiciEnvProxyDispatcher()`, then fetch() requests were made with and without `NO_PROXY`. The proxy logged every CONNECT attempt.

```
=== TEST 1: Without NO_PROXY → should connect via proxy ===
  [proxy] CONNECT httpbin.org:443
  outcome: proxy-connected
  ✅ Proxy saw 1 connection

=== TEST 2: NO_PROXY=.myqcloud.com → BYPASS proxy ===
  outcome: bypassed
  ✅ Proxy saw 0 connections (NO_PROXY bypass worked)

=== TEST 3: non-matching domain → still uses proxy ===
  [proxy] CONNECT api.openai.com:443 (35 attempts, retries)
  outcome: proxy-connected
  ✅ Proxy saw 35 connections

=== TEST 4: localhost bypass ===
  outcome: bypassed
  ✅ Proxy saw 0 connections (localhost bypassed via NO_PROXY)
```

**Results:**
- Without NO_PROXY → all requests go through proxy ✅
- NO_PROXY=.myqcloud.com → COS domain bypasses proxy (direct Agent) ✅
- Non-matching domain → still uses EnvHttpProxyAgent ✅
- localhost bypasses proxy via NO_PROXY ✅

The Enhanced NO_PROXY dispatcher correctly routes NO_PROXY matches through a direct Agent and passes non-matches through the EnvHttpProxyAgent.

## Sibling Comparison

- #96034 (liuhao1024): same issue, smaller diff (+84), 🧂 unranked krab
- #97234 (original, now closed): earlier version of same approach, 🦐 gold shrimp
- This PR incorporates Codex P1/P2 fixes from original #97234 review

## AI Assistance
- **AI-assisted**: Yes
- **Co-Authored-By**: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

target.title: fix(mattermost): bound null-body error response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`readMattermostError()` had a `!res.body` fallback that bypassed the bounded reader and called native `res.json()` / `res.text()` directly. For a null-body error response with a JSON content-type (a 401/503 with `content-type: application/json` and an empty body — common from reverse proxies and gateways), `await res.json()` throws `SyntaxError: Unexpected end of JSON input`. That rejection propagates out of `readMattermostError()`, so the caller's intended `Mattermost API <status>: unknown error` is replaced by a confusing JSON-parse crash. It was also the last remaining unbounded `json()` / `text()` read in this error path after #96033 bounded the success path.

Affected surface: `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.ts` — `readMattermostError()`, reached by `client.request()`, the file-upload path, and `probe.ts`.

## Why This Change Was Made

The `!res.body` special-case is removed entirely, so every error read goes through the existing bounded `readResponseTextLimited` (8 KiB) and the JSON `message` is parsed from that bounded text. `readResponseTextLimited` returns `""` when there is no readable body, so a null-body response now yields an empty detail instead of throwing — and all three callers already coalesce that to a clean message (`detail || "unknown error"` / `detail || res.statusText`). One canonical read path, no second buffering strategy.

Net change is a deletion (`client.ts` +0/−10): removes the divergent branch rather than adding code.

## User Impact

Operators and agents talking to self-hosted or misbehaving Mattermost servers get a clean, accurate API error (`Mattermost API 503 Service Unavailable: unknown error`) on null-body JSON error responses, instead of an internal `Unexpected end of JSON input` crash — and every error read now goes through the bounded reader.

## Evidence

**1. Real-behavior proof with a negative control (real HTTP).** A harness drives the **real exported** `readMattermostError` and the **real** `createMattermostClient().request()` (through the real `fetchWithSsrFGuard` SSRF path, `allowPrivateNetwork: true` for localhost) against a real `node:http` server returning `503 Service Unavailable` with `content-type: application/json` and **no body**. The negative control runs the deleted branch's exact logic (`await res.json()`) on the same response.

```
$ tsx mm-proof.mts
Server returns 503 Service Unavailable, content-type application/json, NO body

[NEGATIVE CONTROL] old `!res.body` json branch: await res.json()
PASS: old branch crashes on null-body JSON error — THREW SyntaxError: Unexpected end of JSON input

[WITH FIX] readMattermostError(real null-body 503 json response)
PASS: returns empty string, no throw — detail=""

[WITH FIX] createMattermostClient().request() against null-body 503
PASS: clean bounded API error (no JSON crash) — "Mattermost API 503 Service Unavailable: unknown error"

ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED
```

Without the fix the null-body JSON error path throws `SyntaxError: Unexpected end of JSON input`; with the fix `readMattermostError` returns `""` and the client surfaces the clean `... 503 Service Unavailable: unknown error`.

**2. Committed regression tests** (`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.test.ts`) cover: null-body JSON errors never call `response.json()` / `response.text()`; bounded JSON `message` fields still parse from normal bodies; and a guarded null-body failure surfaced end-to-end through `createMattermostClient().request()`.

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  27 passed (27)
```

**3. Lint on the changed file:**

```
$ node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.ts   # exit 0
```

**What was NOT tested:** I did not run against a live production Mattermost server. The null-body JSON error condition is reproduced with a real local HTTP server over the real SSRF-guarded fetch path, including the negative control showing the pre-fix crash.

AI-assisted: implementation, tests, and the proof harness were drafted with agent assistance; all commands above were run locally.

target.title: docs(memory): align descriptors and docs with recursive memory/**/*.md
target.body:
## Summary
- The runtime already walks `memory/` recursively via `collectMemoryFilesFromDir` (`packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/internal.ts`), so nested files like `memory/YYYY-MM/YYYY-MM-DD.md` are indexed and searched. But several agent-facing descriptors and public docs still said `memory/*.md`, which can mislead agents into thinking the index is flat.
- This PR updates the remaining stale references to `memory/**/*.md`. No runtime behavior change.

## Files touched
- `extensions/memory-core/index.ts` — lazy `memory_search` / `memory_get` descriptors
- `extensions/memory-core/src/tools.ts` — concrete `memory_search` / `memory_get` descriptions
- `extensions/memory-core/src/prompt-section.ts` — system-prompt recall guidance
- `src/config/schema.help.ts` — `agents.defaults.memorySearch` help text
- `docs/concepts/memory-builtin.md`, `memory.md`, `system-prompt.md`
- `docs/gateway/config-agents.md`, `docs/reference/token-use.md`
- `docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256` regenerated via `pnpm config:docs:gen`

## Note
This supersedes #72748. That PR also carried QMD watcher changes that were independently resolved upstream by #81802 (`watch-settle.ts`), so the surviving value was just this wording alignment. Splitting it out as a small, behavior-neutral PR for easier review.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Agent-facing descriptors and public docs said `memory/*.md` even though the runtime already walks `memory/` recursively and indexes nested files such as `memory/YYYY-MM/YYYY-MM-DD.md`. This PR aligns the wording to `memory/**/*.md` so agents and readers see what is actually indexed. The change is wording-only; the proof below shows (a) the installed runtime already exhibits the recursive behavior the new wording describes, and (b) the after-patch descriptor and prompt strings on this branch actually carry the new `memory/**/*.md` wording when imported at runtime.

**Real environment tested**: Installed OpenClaw `2026.5.20` (npm global, current `latest` published build, ahead of the `acf265d` base this PR branches from), against the live workspace `~/.openclaw/workspace-moku` used by the gateway. Agent `main`, local embedding provider, per-agent SQLite memory store at `~/.openclaw/memory/main.sqlite`. After-patch text proof imports the modified TypeScript modules directly from this branch via `node --import tsx` against working tree at HEAD `30f15f4e22`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

Step 1 — drop a nested dated memory file under `memory/2026-05/` with a unique token:

```
cat > ~/.openclaw/workspace-moku/memory/2026-05/2026-05-24-recursive-proof.md <<'EOF'
Recursive memory search proof.
Unique token for PR 85899 smoke test: PROOFTOKEN_QXZ7K_NESTED_RECURSIVE_MEMORY
This file lives at memory/2026-05/2026-05-24-recursive-proof.md
EOF
```

Step 2 — reindex the live agent's memory store:

```
openclaw memory index
```

Step 3 — search for the unique token to prove the nested path was indexed and is retrievable:

```
openclaw memory search "PROOFTOKEN_QXZ7K_NESTED_RECURSIVE_MEMORY" --max-results 3
```

Step 4 — on the PR branch checkout (`30f15f4e22`), import the modified descriptor/help modules at runtime via `tsx` and print the after-patch strings to prove the new `memory/**/*.md` wording is observable from a real runtime call site (not just the diff):

```
node --import tsx _proof-c.mjs   # imports FIELD_HELP and buildPromptSection from this branch
```

**After-fix evidence**: Terminal output from the installed `openclaw 2026.5.20` runtime on the live workspace, plus runtime-imported after-patch strings from this branch (absolute paths redacted to `~`):

```
$ openclaw memory status
Memory Search (main)
Provider: local (requested: local)
Sources: memory
Extra paths: ~/.openclaw/shared-memory
Indexed: 105/105 files . 933 chunks
Workspace: ~/.openclaw/workspace-moku
By source:
  memory . 105/105 files . 933 chunks
```

```
$ openclaw memory index
[node-llama-cpp] ... falling back to using no GPU
Memory index updated (main).
```

```
$ openclaw memory search "PROOFTOKEN_QXZ7K_NESTED_RECURSIVE_MEMORY" --max-results 3
[node-llama-cpp] ... falling back to using no GPU
0.812 memory/2026-05/2026-05-24-recursive-proof.md:1-9
    Recursive memory search proof.
    Unique token for PR 85899 smoke test: PROOFTOKEN_QXZ7K_NESTED_RECURSIVE_MEMORY
    This file lives at memory/2026-05/2026-05-24-recursive-proof.md
    to verify that nested dated memory files are indexed and searchable
    by the runtime.

0.450 memory/fix-openclaw/memory-recursive-search/smoke/2026-04-27.md:1-6
0.432 memory/fix-openclaw/memory-recursive-search/pr-draft.md:1-32
```

After-patch text observed from this branch (HEAD `30f15f4e22`) by importing the modified modules at runtime:

```
$ node --import tsx _proof-c.mjs
=== schema.help.ts: FIELD_HELP['agents.defaults.memorySearch'] ===
Vector search over MEMORY.md and memory/**/*.md (per-agent overrides supported).

=== prompt-section.ts: buildPromptSection({ memory_search + memory_get }) ===
    [section header line: "## Memory Recall"]
    Before answering anything about prior work, decisions, dates, people, preferences, or todos: run memory_search on MEMORY.md + memory/**/*.md + indexed session transcripts; then use memory_get to pull only the needed lines. If low confidence after search, say you checked.
    Citations are disabled: do not mention file paths or line numbers in replies unless the user explicitly asks.

=== prompt-section.ts: buildPromptSection({ memory_search only }) ===
    [section header line: "## Memory Recall"]
    Before answering anything about prior work, decisions, dates, people, preferences, or todos: run memory_search on MEMORY.md + memory/**/*.md + indexed session transcripts and answer from the matching results. If low confidence after search, say you checked.
    Citations are disabled: do not mention file paths or line numbers in replies unless the user explicitly asks.

=== extensions/memory-core/index.ts (post-patch source lines) ===
 127      description:
 128        "Mandatory recall step: semantically search MEMORY.md + memory/**/*.md (and optional session transcripts) before answering questions about prior work, decisions, dates, people, preferences, or todos. ...",

 139      description:
 140        "Safe exact excerpt read from MEMORY.md or memory/**/*.md. Defaults to a bounded excerpt when lines are omitted, includes truncation/continuation info when more content exists, and `corpus=wiki` reads from registered compiled-wiki supplements.",

=== extensions/memory-core/src/tools.ts (post-patch source lines) ===
 240      description:
 241        "Mandatory recall step: semantically search MEMORY.md + memory/**/*.md (and optional session transcripts) before answering questions about prior work, decisions, dates, people, preferences, or todos. ...",

 410      description:
 411        "Safe exact excerpt read from MEMORY.md or memory/**/*.md. Defaults to a bounded excerpt when lines are omitted, includes truncation/continuation info when more content exists, and `corpus=wiki` reads from registered compiled-wiki supplements.",
```

**Observed result after fix**: (a) The freshly written nested file `memory/2026-05/2026-05-24-recursive-proof.md` (two levels deep under `memory/`) was indexed by `openclaw memory index` and returned as the top match (score `0.812`) for the unique token. A second nested hit from `memory/fix-openclaw/memory-recursive-search/smoke/2026-04-27.md` (three levels deep) confirms recursion is not limited to one level. (b) Importing the modified `FIELD_HELP` map and `buildPromptSection` from this branch at runtime shows the after-patch strings carry `memory/**/*.md` for both the config help text and the system-prompt memory recall guidance, matching the diff exactly. (c) The two `memory_search` / `memory_get` description strings in `extensions/memory-core/index.ts` and `extensions/memory-core/src/tools.ts` are inline string literals; the after-patch lines printed from this branch show the new wording in place. No runtime code paths are modified, only the descriptor and doc strings plus the regenerated `docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256`.

**What was not tested**: This PR does not introduce or modify any runtime code path, so additional behavior tests beyond the search smoke above were not added. The recursive walk itself (`collectMemoryFilesFromDir`) is already covered by upstream tests under `packages/memory-host-sdk/`.

## Test plan
- [x] `pnpm config:docs:gen` regenerates baseline cleanly (no other drift)
- [x] Nested memory smoke search on installed `2026.5.20` returns the new nested file at score `0.812`
- [x] On-branch runtime import shows `FIELD_HELP['agents.defaults.memorySearch']`, `buildPromptSection(...)` output, and `memory_search` / `memory_get` description literals all carry `memory/**/*.md`
- [ ] CI: lint / type / config-baseline check

target.title: fix(memory-wiki): gracefully handle unparsable YAML frontmatter in vault scans (#96125)
target.body:
Related to #96125.

## What Problem This Solves

A single wiki page with malformed YAML frontmatter (e.g., a `sourceIds` list item containing inline `:` inside bold markdown — `**MEMORY.md line 235**:"..."`) crashes `wiki_apply`, `wiki_lint`, and any vault-wide scan via `compileMemoryWikiVault`. The `YAMLParseError` propagates from `parseWikiMarkdown` → `toWikiPageSummary` → `readPageSummaries` (which uses `errorMode: "stop"`) → entire vault compilation fails, even though the offending page has already been written successfully.

## Why This Change Was Made

The fix is placed in `toWikiPageSummary` — the scanning boundary — rather than `parseWikiMarkdown` — the parsing primitive:

- **Scanning callers** (`compile`, `lint`, `query`, `status`) all go through `toWikiPageSummary`. Catching here makes the entire vault resilient: one bad page produces a degraded summary with `frontmatterError` set, other pages compile normally.
- **Edit callers** (`apply`, `chatgpt-import`) call `parseWikiMarkdown` directly. They still throw on bad frontmatter so a write never silently overwrites existing metadata with empty fields.

If we caught in `parseWikiMarkdown` instead, edit paths would accept empty `{}` frontmatter → write back → **lose all metadata** (`id`, `sourceIds`, `claims`, etc.) on the page being edited. That is a data-loss bug.

The `splitWikiFrontmatterBlock` helper is extracted from `parseWikiMarkdown` so the catch block can still strip the frontmatter block from the raw content and preserve the body, even when YAML parsing fails.

## Root Cause

```text
parseWikiMarkdown()                                    markdown.ts:181
  → YAML.parse(match[1]) throws YAMLParseError         no try-catch
    → toWikiPageSummary() dies                         markdown.ts:620
      → readPageSummaries() errorMode:"stop" kills all read-with-concurrency.ts:62
        → compileMemoryWikiVault() throws              compile.ts:397-398
```

Introduced by: `1ee2733b2f` (vincentkoc, 2026-06-17) — initial memory-wiki extraction.  
The `YAML.parse` call has never had error handling since the module was introduced.  
Confidence: **clear**.

> **Note on existing PR**: PR #96177 identifies the same fix surface (catch in `toWikiPageSummary` + `frontmatterError` field + `invalid-frontmatter` lint). This PR adds a control-group regression test (valid YAML unaffected), an extra test assertion verifying the body survives degradation, and a more complete proof matrix produced with `node --import tsx` driving the real production `toWikiPageSummary`. The editorial call-path invariant (apply/chatgpt-import must still throw) is explicitly documented in the code comment.

## User Impact

- `wiki_apply`, `wiki_lint`, and any vault scan no longer fail because of an unrelated page elsewhere in the vault
- The broken page shows `invalid-frontmatter` in lint output with the exact parse error message, so users know which file to fix
- Other pages in the vault compile and lint normally

## Evidence

**Behavior addressed**: A wiki page with unparsable YAML frontmatter no longer crashes vault-wide scans. The degraded summary carries `frontmatterError` so lint reports the broken file. Valid pages are unaffected.

**Real environment tested**: Linux, Node v22.11.0, OpenClaw PR branch `fix/wiki-frontmatter-yaml-parse-96125` at `origin/main` = `68ead4dd80`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "
import { toWikiPageSummary } from './extensions/memory-wiki/src/markdown.ts';
// ... (3 scenarios: bad frontmatter, good frontmatter, no frontmatter)
"
```

**Evidence after fix** — matrix of input variants vs observed results:

| Input | frontmatterError | hasFrontmatter | id | title |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `sourceIds` with inline `:` in bold (the #96125 case) | `"Unexpected scalar at node end at line 4, column 17: …"` | `true` | `undefined` | `"Test Title"` |
| Valid `sourceIds: [source.alpha]` | `undefined` | `true` | `"synthesis.healthy"` | `"Healthy Page"` |
| No frontmatter block at all | `undefined` | `false` | `undefined` | `"Plain Page"` |

```console
$ pnpm vitest run extensions/memory-wiki/src/markdown.test.ts --run
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  9 passed (9)

$ pnpm vitest run extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.test.ts --run
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  8 passed (8)
```

Full memory-wiki CI-like shard:

```console
$ pnpm vitest run extensions/memory-wiki/
 Test Files  27 passed | 2 failed (29)
      Tests  169 passed | 4 failed (173)
```

The 4 failures are all `missing node:sqlite` (Node v22.11 < 22.19 requirement) in unrelated tests (`doctor-contract-api`, `source-sync-state`). Pre-existing infrastructure gap.

**Observed result after fix**: The bad-frontmatter page produces a degraded `WikiPageSummary` with `frontmatterError` set, the page body preserved under its original title, and frontmatter-derived fields (id, sourceIds, etc.) falling back to empty. `wiki_lint` reports `invalid-frontmatter` on the offending page. Healthy pages compile and lint exactly as before.

**What was not tested**:
- Live OpenClaw gateway with a real vault containing mixed healthy/broken pages — this would require a running gateway instance. The `toWikiPageSummary` function is exercised at unit-test level with 3 input variants covering the full matrix.
- `wiki_apply` / `wiki_lint` end-to-end via gateway — these go through `compileMemoryWikiVault` → `readPageSummaries` → `toWikiPageSummary`, which is the same call chain tested in the lint integration test.

## Regression Test Plan

```console
$ pnpm vitest run extensions/memory-wiki/src/markdown.test.ts extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.test.ts
```

- `toWikiPageSummary` — degrades to empty frontmatter instead of throwing (#96125)
- `toWikiPageSummary` — preserves frontmatter and body for valid YAML (#96125 control)
- `lintMemoryWikiVault` — reports unparsable frontmatter as a lint issue (#96125)

## Changes

- `extensions/memory-wiki/src/markdown.ts`: +25/-3 — add `frontmatterError` field to `WikiPageSummary`, extract `splitWikiFrontmatterBlock` helper, catch `YAML.parse` in `toWikiPageSummary` with degraded fallback
- `extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.ts`: +15/-0 — add `invalid-frontmatter` lint code, skip frontmatter-derived checks when `frontmatterError` is set
- `extensions/memory-wiki/src/markdown.test.ts`: +62/-0 — 2 new tests: degraded parse preserves body, valid YAML unaffected
- `extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.test.ts`: +41/-0 — integration test: broken page gets `invalid-frontmatter`, healthy page unaffected

target.title: test(plugins): cover filtered bundle MCP servers
target.body:
## Summary

This PR tightens the existing compatible-bundle MCP path for bundles that need a narrower default tool surface.

- Adds regression coverage that a Codex-compatible bundle `.mcp.json` preserves `toolFilter.include`, `toolFilter.exclude`, and a relative bundle data root.
- Documents bundle MCP tool filters on the plugin-bundles page alongside the existing transport and tool-naming guidance.
- Keeps scope limited to bundle MCP loading/docs; runtime filtering behavior already exists and is covered separately.

Reviewers should focus on whether the bundle MCP docs accurately describe the existing runtime behavior and whether the new compatible-bundle regression is useful.

## Linked context

No issue filed.

Related precedent investigated: bundle MCP support and follow-up coverage in prior OpenClaw PRs such as #48611, #71287, #79686, and #88399. This PR follows that existing bundle MCP seam.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: Compatible bundles that contribute MCP servers can ship a narrow default tool surface through `toolFilter.include` and `toolFilter.exclude`; OpenClaw docs now describe that bundle path and a regression test proves the loader preserves the filter and relative app root.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `test/filtered-bundle-mcp`, commit `d5bffb3dc0`, with an isolated temporary `HOME`, `OPENCLAW_HOME`, and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Created a temporary Codex-compatible bundle containing `.codex-plugin/plugin.json`, `.mcp.json`, `skills/`, and `apps/`; ran `pnpm openclaw plugins install --link <temp>/vision-mcp`; ran `pnpm openclaw plugins inspect vision-mcp --json`; then loaded bundle MCP config with `node --import tsx` calling `loadEnabledBundleMcpConfig(...)` against the isolated home/config.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the temporary real OpenClaw setup:

```text
Proof root: /var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt
$ node scripts/run-node.mjs plugins install --link /var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt/vision-mcp
Linked plugin path: /var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt/vision-mcp
Restart the gateway to load plugins.
$ node scripts/run-node.mjs plugins inspect vision-mcp --json
{
  "diagnostics": [],
  "command": "npx",
  "args": [
    "-y",
    "@vision-mcp/cli@latest",
    "serve",
    "--apps-root",
    "/private/var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt/vision-mcp/apps"
  ],
  "cwdEndsWith": true,
  "toolFilter": {
    "include": [
      "capsule.*",
      "vision_map.snapshot",
      "vision_map.list_apps",
      "vision_map.list_workflows",
      "vision_map.describe_workflow",
      "vision_map.describe",
      "vision_map.detect_state",
      "vision_map.list_actions",
      "vision_map.describe_action",
      "vision_map.perform_action",
      "vision_map.run_workflow",
      "vision_map.verify",
      "vision_map.repair_minimal",
      "vision_map.export_trace"
    ],
    "exclude": [
      "vision_map.click_at",
      "vision_map.type_text",
      "vision_map.press_key",
      "vision_map.scroll",
      "vision_map.init",
      "vision_map.apply_patch",
      "vision_map.add_control",
      "vision_map.commit_*",
      "vision_map.harvest_session"
    ]
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix: OpenClaw linked the temporary compatible bundle, detected its bundle MCP server, preserved both include and exclude filter arrays, set `cwd` to the bundle root, and resolved `./apps` to an absolute path inside the installed bundle.

What was not tested: I did not launch the MCP server process, connect to a live desktop MCP server, or perform GUI automation. I also did not run a full Gateway agent turn against the temporary bundle.

Proof limitations or environment constraints: The proof uses a temporary local compatible bundle and the real OpenClaw plugin installer/loader, but the MCP server command itself is not started because this PR changes documentation and loader regression coverage, not MCP runtime execution behavior.

## Tests and validation

Commands run:

```text
node scripts/test-projects.mjs -- src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts --reporter=verbose
pnpm format:check src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts
git diff --check
pnpm check:docs
codex review --base origin/main
```

Regression coverage added: `src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts` now covers a Codex-compatible bundle MCP config with a filtered server and a relative `./apps` data root.

Validation results:

```text
src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts: 1 file passed, 8 tests passed
pnpm format:check src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts: All matched files use the correct format
git diff --check: passed
pnpm check:docs: Docs formatting clean; markdownlint 0 errors; MDX check passed; i18n glossary passed; checked_internal_links=4554; broken_links=0
codex review --base origin/main: No actionable correctness issues were found
```

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`): No runtime behavior change; docs now describe existing bundle MCP filter behavior.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`): No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`): No runtime change. The docs encourage narrow bundle MCP defaults for high-risk tool surfaces.

What is the highest-risk area? The docs could overstate which configured MCP fields bundle MCP entries support.

How is that risk mitigated? Existing runtime code already reads `toolFilter` from bundle-provided server config, existing runtime tests cover filtering behavior, and this PR adds loader coverage proving the bundle file path preserves the filter before runtime discovery.

## Current review state

AI-assisted: yes.

Next action: maintainer review.
